claims.claim: When SOC ≥ 50%, the thermal runaway reaction of a lithium-ion battery will be more intense and its internal temperature will exceed 900°C.
claims.quote: when SOC ≥ 50 %, the thermal runaway reaction of the battery will be more intense, and its internal temperature will exceed 900 C.

claims.claim: The opening temperature of the pressure relief valve of a 100% SOC battery is between 130°C and 150°C.
claims.quote: the opening temperature of the pressure relief valve of the 100 % SOC battery is between 130 C and 150 C

claims.claim: The maximum temperature difference between the inside and outside of the battery during thermal runaway at 100% SOC reaches 429.8°C.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature difference between the inside and outside of the thermal runaway reaches 429.8 C

claims.claim: The maximum internal temperature recorded during thermal runaway in the experiment was 1063.2°C, with all peak temperatures remaining below 1100°C.
claims.quote: The maximum internal temperature recorded during thermal runaway in the experiment was 1063.2 C, with all peak temperatures remaining below 1100 C.

claims.claim: The opening temperature of the pressure relief valve of the aged battery was found to be lower than that of the new battery.
claims.quote: the opening temperature of the pressure relief valve of the aged battery was found to be lower than that of the new battery

claims.claim: For a 0% SOC battery undergoing thermal runaway, the maximum external temperature range was 390°C to 730°C and the maximum internal temperature range was 480°C to 1020°C.
claims.quote: The maximum external temperature range during thermal runaway was 390 C to 730 C, while the maximum internal temperature range was 480 C to 1020 C.

claims.claim: At 50% SOC, the internal temperature reached approximately 1017.8°C with a maximum internal temperature rise rate of 297.3°C/s during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the internal temperature of the battery rapidly rose to 1017.8 C with a maximum internal temperature rise rate recorded at 297.3 C/s

claims.claim: An aging battery with SOH of 0.8 subjected to 300 W heating experienced explosion, resulting in the battery fragmenting into two pieces, whereas fresh batteries at 300 W did not exhibit this behavior.
claims.quote: In the thermal runaway experiment of the aging battery, an explosion occurred when subjected to 300 W heating, resulting in the fragmentation of the battery into two pieces.

claims.claim: Higher heating power does not necessarily result in higher internal temperatures during battery thermal runaway, because the internal temperature is influenced by both external heating power and internal chain reactions.
claims.quote: It is important to note that for battery thermal runaway events, higher heating power doesn't necessarily result in higher internal temperatures. The internal temperature during battery thermal runaway is influenced not only by external heating power, but also by internal chain reactions and material combustion characteristics within the battery itself.

claims.claim: Gas concentration monitoring can only detect obvious gas concentration after the battery pressure relief valve is loosened, meaning the battery has already faced thermal runaway before detection.
claims.quote: For gas monitoring and early warning, the obvious gas concentration can only be detected after the battery pressure relief valve is loosened, and the battery has already faced thermal runaway.

claims.claim: SEI decomposition, separator melting, and internal short circuit occur successively at internal temperatures of 90–150°C in 18650 batteries.
claims.quote: Mei et al. [5] carried out thermally induced runaway experiments on 18,650 batteries, and proposed that SEI decomposition, separator melting, and internal short circuit occurred successively at internal temperatures of 90–150 C.

claims.claim: The first-level thermal runaway warning threshold is set at an external temperature of 82°C, corresponding to the SEI decomposition internal temperature of 80°C.
claims.quote: the SEI decomposition temperature (Tin = 80 C) is selected as the first-level warning temperature. According to the battery internal temperature prediction model, the first-level warning external temperature is 82 C.

claims.claim: The second-level thermal runaway warning threshold is set at an external temperature of 92°C, corresponding to the internal temperature rise rate acceleration point of Tin = 100°C before the pressure relief valve opens.
claims.quote: the internal temperature rise rate acceleration growth point (Tin = 100 C) before the opening of the pressure relief valve is selected as the secondary warning temperature... the second-level warning external temperature is 92 C.

claims.claim: Aging batteries demonstrated significantly higher maximum external temperatures during thermal runaway compared to fresh batteries, indicating a more intensified thermal runaway reaction in aged cells.
claims.quote: the aging batteries demonstrates significantly higher external temperatures than the fresh batteries... The appearance of these characteristics depicted in Fig. 7(c) suggests that the aging batteries experienced a more intensified thermal runaway reaction.

claims.claim: Embedding a thermocouple inside the battery by drilling caused a decrease of 0.4% in charge capacity, 0.8% in discharge capacity, 3.0% in charge internal resistance, and 3.2% in discharge internal resistance.
claims.quote: The results presented in Table 2 demonstrate a decrease of 0.4 %, 0.8 %, 3.0 %, and 3.2 % in the capacity and internal resistance of battery charging and discharging after it was implanted with the TC.

claims.claim: The 300 Ah LFP battery did not self-ignite even when the surface temperature reached over 600°C during preliminary testing.
claims.quote: It found that this LFP battery did not self-ignite even when the surface temperature reached over 600°C.

claims.claim: Flaming combustion accelerates the occurrence of thermal runaway in a single LFP cell but has little influence on the peak surface temperature.
claims.quote: the flaming combustion can accelerate the occurrence of TR of a single cell, but the peak surface temperature is mainly determined by the cell's internal heat generation and less influenced by the flaming combustion.

claims.claim: Mass loss after thermal runaway increases with SOC but is not influenced by whether the cell is ignited or not.
claims.quote: The mass loss after TR increases with SOC, according to Test #3, #4 and #5. On the other hand, there is no strict relationship between the mass loss and the occurrence of ignition.

claims.claim: For the 100% SOC LFP cell without flame, the minimum fresh air renewal rate required to keep CO concentration below 667 ppm in an 80 m³ garage with 10 combusting cells is 107 h⁻¹.
claims.quote: For the 100% SOC cell without combustion, the minimum number of fresh air renewal per hour τ (h⁻¹) is required to be 107 to contain the CO concentration lower than 667 ppm.

claims.claim: The fractional effective dose (XFED) for a 10-cell pack combusting in an 80 m³ garage at a fresh air renewal rate of 4 h⁻¹ is much greater than the critical value of 1, indicating catastrophic toxicity.
claims.quote: The XFED is much greater than the critical value of 1 (under the selected air renewal ratio), indicating that the fire-induced toxicity of a 10-cell pack is very serious.

claims.claim: Peak heat release rate drops sharply from 88.78 kW at 100% SOC to 16.73 kW at 50% SOC, while the difference between 50% and 20% SOC is relatively small at approximately 4 kW.
claims.quote: As SOC decreases, the peak HRR decreases sharply especially from 88.78 kW of 100% SOC to 16.73 kW of 50% SOC. While the difference between the peak HRRs of 50% and 20% SOC cells are relatively small, only 4 kW.

claims.claim: Total heat release values for 100%, 50%, and 20% SOC cells are similar at 16238, 16138, and 13568 kJ respectively, indicating combustion heat is mainly determined by the amount of flammable components inside the cell.
claims.quote: The similar THRs for different SOC cells indicate that the combustion heat of this large-scale LIB is mainly determined by the amount of the flammable components inside the cell.

claims.claim: The cooling effect of safety valve opening on each thermocouple is influenced by the distribution of electrolyte inside the cell; the more electrolyte near a thermocouple site, the larger the temperature decrease due to electrolyte boiling.
claims.quote: The cooling effect of the safety valve opening on each thermocouple is influenced by the distribution of electrolyte inside the cell. The more electrolyte near the thermocouple site, the larger temperature decrease there for the heat absorption of electrolyte boiling.

claims.claim: The TR expanding process from the heated front surface to the back surface is clearly observable in the 300 Ah large-size cell but may not be observable in smaller test samples.
claims.quote: This TR expanding is especially obvious for the large size LIB in this study and maybe can't be observed in other studies when the test samples are small.

claims.claim: When an LIB's electrolyte solvent flame is extinguished without cooling the battery body, the battery can still undergo thermal runaway with more aggressive release of flammable gases and heat.
claims.quote: If the flame of the electrolyte solvent is put out, but the battery is not cooled down, then it could still fall into TR with a more aggressive release of flammable gases and heat.

claims.claim: For 100% SOC cells without flame (Test #6), the net CO generation from internal battery reactions reaches 42.8 g, compared to only 2.2 g for 20% SOC cells without flame (Test #7).
claims.quote: the net generation of CO by the LIB's internal reaction reaches 42.8 g at 100% SOC in Test #6, while the 20% SOC cell only generates 2.2 g CO in Test #7.

claims.claim: The peak HRR of the 100% SOC 300 Ah LFP cell (88.78 kW) is equivalent to the pool fire of methanol with a diameter of 0.61 m.
claims.quote: For the 100% SOC cell, its peak HRR is equivalent to the pool fire of methanol with 0.61 m in diameter.

claims.claim: When water spray was applied to a burning 21700 LIB, the concentration of CO, H2, and HF increased while CO2 decreased compared to combustion without water spray.
claims.quote: When water was applied, the maximum concentration of CO, H2 and HF increased while CO2 decreased.

claims.claim: Water spray contact efficiency with batteries decreased with increasing water pressure, because higher pressure reduced spray duration and therefore continuous cooling effectiveness.
claims.quote: The contact efficiency between the water spray and batteries decreased with the increase in water pressure.

claims.claim: With sufficient water volume (1.05 L at 0.2 MPa) or sufficiently low pressure (0.7 L at 0.1 MPa), water spray prevented thermal runaway propagation in a 2×2 multi-cell 21700 battery configuration.
claims.quote: Application of 1.05 kg of water at 0.2 MPa (Test 10) or 0.7 L of water at 0.1 MPa (Test 11) effectively prevents the propagation of thermal runaway, while others fail, only delaying the onset of thermal runaway in the third cell.

claims.claim: HF concentration increased when water was applied during thermal runaway because water reacts with PF5 (a decomposition product of LiPF6) to produce HF and phosphoryl fluoride (PF3O).
claims.quote: When water is applied, the water reacts with PF5 to produce HF and phosphoryl fluoride (PF3O). So the concentration of HF increased after water spraying.

claims.claim: With insufficient water volume or low contact efficiency, water spray delayed the time to onset of thermal runaway propagation but did not prevent it.
claims.quote: With insufficient volume of water spray or low contact efficiency, the time to onset of thermal runaway was delayed.

claims.claim: The cooling rate after temperature recovery showed no significant difference across water spray conditions tested, despite differences in maximum temperature reached.
claims.quote: Water spray can effectively reduce the maximum temperature with no significant difference in the cooling rate after the water spray stops.

claims.claim: For similar lithium-ion battery-based systems, the recommended spray intensity is 207 L min⁻¹ m⁻² and the recommended water quantity is 20.8 g W⁻¹ h⁻¹ at 0.1 MPa water pressure.
claims.quote: the proper spray intensity is 207 L min−1 m−2 and the proper water quantity is 20.8 g W−1 h−1, with 0.1 MPa water pressure.

claims.claim: For a LiCoO2/graphite pouch battery at 95% SOC under mechanical indentation, thermal runaway is activated when temperature reaches approximately 150°C, causing rapid temperature rise to about 650°C.
claims.quote: As the temperature increases and reaches the critical point at around 150°C, the battery usher in a sharp heat generation peak and rapid thermal surge, at which point thermal runaway reaction is activated within a short period of time. The temperature rises rapidly to about 650°C, causing the separator to collapse over a large area and the active materials and the current collector to melt.

claims.claim: For the LiCoO2/graphite pouch battery studied, a SOC of 0.6 is the critical point for triggering thermal runaway under mechanical indentation, though this value may vary with battery structure and material system.
claims.quote: For the batteries considered in this study, a SOC of 0.6 is the critical point for triggering thermal runaway; however, this value may vary with the battery structure and material system.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of a high-SOC LiCoO2/graphite pouch cell under mechanical crush, SEI decomposition occurs first at 130°C, followed by anode-electrolyte reaction at 150°C, cathode-electrolyte reaction at approximately 184°C, and electrolyte decomposition at approximately 230°C.
claims.quote: SEI decomposition occurs for the first of the four reactions (the SEI decomposition, the cathode-electrolyte reaction, the cathode-electrolyte reaction and the electrolyte decomposition) at 130°C, followed by the anode-electrolyte reaction at 150°C. The cathode-electrolyte reaction, which is the most severe exothermic reaction, as shown in Fig. 7b, occurs at around 184°C, and the electrolyte decomposition reaction occurs at approximately 230°C.

claims.claim: The anode-electrolyte reaction is the key contributor to thermal runaway heat generation and determines the triggering of violent thermal runaway, while the cathode-electrolyte reaction manifests the highest exothermic peak among the four reactions.
claims.quote: Among these, the anode-electrolyte reaction is the key contributor for thermal runaway, as it contributes the most heat generation and determines the triggering of the violent thermal runaway, while the cathode-electrolyte reaction manifests the highest exothermic peak among the four reactions.

claims.claim: In nail penetration of a pouch battery, the coupled model predicts thermal runaway origination at approximately 128°C within approximately 3 seconds, with heat propagating outward from the nail site which always exhibits the maximum temperature throughout the penetration stage.
claims.quote: The coupled model traces out the origination of the thermal runaway at approximately 128°C within approximately 3 s for the simulation case, as shown in Fig. 8c... the heat propagates to surrounding areas from the short-circuit location after nail penetration, and the maximum temperature is always found at the nail site (the short-circuit location) throughout the penetration stage.

claims.claim: For mechanically induced internal short circuit, the short circuit serves only as an inducing factor for thermal abuse with limited heat generation contribution, while the anode-electrolyte reaction provides the primary drastic exothermic contribution to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: For mechanically induced safety issues, the internal short circuit shows limited contribution to heat generation and serve as an inducing factor for thermal abuse in lithium-ion batteries. However, the anode-electrolyte reaction provides the prior chance for a drastic exothermic reaction and contributes most of the heat generation for thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Transport limitation of Li+ concentration in the solid phase during internal short circuit may be responsible for the sudden decrease in voltage and short current, but for most high-energy batteries enough heat has already accumulated to trigger thermal runaway before this transport limitation takes effect.
claims.quote: This transport limitation of the Li+ in the solid phase may be responsible for the sudden decrease in voltage and short current during the internal short circuit process. However, it should be noted that, for most high-energy batteries, enough heat energy has been accumulated and triggered thermal runaway before the transport limitation begins to take effect.

claims.claim: Within a certain range, a larger cross-sectional area of the penetrating nail may lead to incomplete energy release, resulting in a relatively lower temperature rise compared to when a finer needle is used.
claims.quote: our research found that within a certain range, a larger cross-sectional area of the nail may lead to an incomplete release of energy; in such a case, the temperature rise will be relatively lower than that when a finer needle is used in the test.

claims.claim: When heating power is the same, a smaller heating area with higher heating power density can trigger thermal runaway quicker, and the heater produces less heating energy with less flux energy introduced into the battery.
claims.quote: For the same heating power, smaller heating area had higher heating power density could trigger TR quicker. Then less heating power would be produced, and less flux energy would be introduced into the battery.

claims.claim: When heating power density is the same, a larger heating area will trigger thermal runaway faster because larger heating area signifies a larger heating power.
claims.quote: The larger heating area will trigger TR faster when heating power density is the same, because larger heating area signifies a larger heating power.

claims.claim: The heating scheme combining both high heating power and small heating area has the greatest ability to shorten the heating time to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The scheme with both high heating power and small heating area is that works most for shortening the heating time.

claims.claim: At low heating power (150 W), 2.46 × 10^5 J of flux energy is needed to trigger thermal runaway with a heating time of 1956 s and an equivalent flux power of 125.8 W, whereas at high heating power (1250 W), only 0.45 × 10^5 J of flux energy is needed with a heating time of 79 s and an equivalent flux power of 570 W.
claims.quote: At low heating power, like 150 W, 2.46 × 10e5 J flux energy is needed to trigger TR, the heating time is 1956s, the equivalent flux power is 125.8W. While at high heating power, like 1250 W, only 0.45 × 10e5J flux energy is needed to trigger TR, the heating time is 79s, the equivalent flux power is 570 W.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway induced by external heating is mainly determined by the balance of flux power from the heater and the heat conduction power of the battery core: at low heating power the battery core conducts most of the flux power and heating lasts a long time, while at high heating power the front part of the battery core quickly reaches the thermal runaway temperature T2.
claims.quote: Thermal runaway induced by external heating method is mainly determined by the balance of the flux power from the heater and the heat conduction power of battery core. When at low heating power, ΔPeqv is very small, the battery core itself conducts most of the flux power from the heater. Heating process is going to last a long time. When at high heating power, ΔPeqv becomes so large that the front part of the battery core can soon reach the thermal runaway temperature T2.

claims.claim: The precision of the 3D thermal runaway model for predicting thermal runaway time, validated against eight external heating tests, was on average larger than 85%.
claims.quote: The precision of the model for predicting TR was averagely larger than 85%, and the precision analyses were listed in Table 5.

claims.claim: Increasing PCM thermal conductivity reduces battery temperature during normal operation but accelerates heat transfer between batteries during thermal runaway, and when PCM thermal conductivity reaches 0.8 W/(m·K), the temperature of the adjacent battery exceeds the critical thermal runaway temperature causing propagation.
claims.quote: The maximum battery temperatures are 93.3 ℃, 140.2 ℃ and 171.5 ℃ with the PCM thermal conductivity of 0.2 W/(m.K), 0.4 W/(m.K), and 0.6 W/(m.K), respectively. They are gradually close to the irreversible temperature of thermal runaway. When the PCM thermal conductivity increases to 0.8 W/(m.K), the temperature of battery 2 exceeds the critical temperature, and thermal runaway occurs.

claims.claim: In the PCM-only system (system 1), thermal runaway propagation from battery 1 to battery 2 is delayed but not prevented, with a thermal runaway time interval of 460 seconds between the two batteries.
claims.quote: Thus, the system 1 is unable to prevent thermal runaway propagation effectively. However, the thermal runaway effectively slows down due to the heat absorption and insulation effect of PCM. Form the heat generation rates of these two batteries, it can be seen that the thermal runaway time interval between the two batteries is 460 s.

claims.claim: In the aluminum plate and water-cooling only system (system 2), thermal runaway occurs almost simultaneously in both batteries due to the lack of effective heat insulation between them.
claims.quote: In system 2, as shown in Fig. 9 (b), when the thermal runaway occurs in battery 1, the heat can quickly transfer between the two batteries, and the temperature of battery rises fast due to the lack of effective heat insulation between the two batteries... thermal runaway occurs almost simultaneously in both two batteries.

claims.claim: When PCM thermal conductivity is 0.8 W/(m·K), a cooling water velocity of 0.05 m/s is required to avoid thermal runaway propagation, whereas at PCM thermal conductivity of 0.4 or 0.6 W/(m·K), a water velocity of 0.01 m/s is sufficient.
claims.quote: The cooling water velocity of 0.01 m/s can avoid the occurrence of thermal runaway propagation as the PCM thermal conductivity is 0.4 W/(m.K) or 0.6 W/(m.K). Once the PCM thermal conductivity increases to 0.8 W/(m.K), the velocity of cooling water needs to be increased to 0.05 m/s to avoid thermal runaway propagation.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway model treats the process as irreversible when battery temperature reaches 230°C, and incorporates four decomposition reactions: SEI decomposition (onset ≥70°C), negative-electrolyte reaction (onset ≥120°C), positive-electrolyte reaction (onset ≥200°C), and electrolyte decomposition (onset ≥230°C).
claims.quote: It is considered that the thermal runaway is irreversible when the temperature (bT) of the battery reaches 230℃... Solid electrolyte interface (SEI) decomposition, (bT ≥ 70℃)... Reaction of negative-electrolyte, (bT ≥ 120℃)... Reaction of positive-electrolyte (bT ≥ 200℃)... Electrolyte decomposition (bT ≥ 230℃)

claims.claim: The PCM-based BTMS alone (system 1) controls battery temperature difference within 1°C during 5C discharge, while the aluminum plate and water-cooling system (system 2) produces a temperature difference of 14.7°C, which far exceeds the required limit of 5°C.
claims.quote: the PCM-based BTMS (system 1 and system 3) can control the temperature difference of the battery within 1 ℃, which meets the working requirements of the power battery system well. However, the temperature difference of the battery under the aluminum and water-cooling coupled BTMS reaches 14.7 ℃, which far exceeds the temperature difference required by the power battery system.

claims.claim: When cooling water velocity is increased to 0.01 m/s with PCM thermal conductivity of 0.2 W/(m·K), the maximum temperature of battery 2 is controlled below 93.3°C, which is 63.5°C lower than the case when the water-cooling system does not start.
claims.quote: when the thermal conductivity is 0.2 W/(m.K), the battery 2 can obtain good cooling conditions when the cooling water velocity increases to 0.01 m/s. The maximum temperature of battery 2 can be controlled below 93.3 ℃, which is 63.5 ℃ lower than the case when the water-cooling system does not start.

claims.claim: From 2014 to the first half of 2019, more than 14% of electric vehicle accidents were caused by vehicle collision and compression.
claims.quote: from 2014 to the first half of 2019, more than 14% of electric vehicle accidents were caused by vehicle collision and compression

claims.claim: Under quasi-static conditions, the electrolyte has little influence on the compression process, but under dynamic compression the electrolyte greatly increases battery strength.
claims.quote: the electrolyte under quasi-static condition has little influence on the compression process but will greatly increase the battery strength under dynamic compression

claims.claim: Under dynamic compression, the deformation behavior of STAGE IV is significantly different from that under quasi-static compression due to damage and energy absorption of the battery shell casing.
claims.quote: Under the dynamic compression, the deformation behavior of STAGE IV is significantly different from that of quasi-static compression due to the damage and energy absorption of battery shell casing.

claims.claim: When the load rate increases by 120 times, the average increase in equivalent strength of the two batteries in the elastic phase is only 6% and 25%, respectively.
claims.quote: the load rate increases by 120 times, the average increase in the equivalent strength of the two batteries is only 6% and 25%, respectively

claims.claim: Under the hemispherical punch test and rigid rod test, the mechanical soft short-circuit occurs 0.88 mm and 0.75 mm ahead of the electrical soft short-circuit, respectively.
claims.quote: the mechanical soft-short circuit is 0.88 mm and 0.75 mm ahead of the electrical soft short-circuit, respectively, and significantly ahead of the thermal soft short-circuit

claims.claim: When sensors detect electrical and thermal failure signals of a battery, the battery may have already entered an irreversible thermal runaway stage.
claims.quote: When the sensors detect electrical and thermal failure signal of battery, it means that the battery may have already entered an irreversible thermal runaway

claims.claim: The failure of one layer of separator leads to a sudden increase of force on the adjacent separator, and the cascading effect makes the multi-layer battery structure fail almost simultaneously.
claims.quote: the failure of one layer of separator will lead to a sudden increase of the force to the adjacent separator, and the cascading effect makes the multi-layer battery structure fail almost simultaneously

claims.claim: The force of the shell casing under lateral compression accounts for only 0.3%–0.6% of the battery peak force.
claims.quote: the force of the shell casing under the lateral compression only accounts for 0.3%-0.6% of the battery peak force

claims.claim: At high strain rate (load rate of 60 mm min-1), the equivalent strength of both Sony and Samsung batteries tends to be consistent.
claims.quote: when the load rate reaches 60 mm min-1, the equivalent strength of the two batteries tends to be consistent

claims.claim: It is insufficient to predict battery failure using only voltage and temperature signals, especially for large-format cells, because SOC will not change immediately when internal structure is damaged.
claims.quote: it is insufficient to predict the failure of battery only by voltage and temperature signals, especially for the large-format cells. When the internal structure is damaged, the SOC will not change immediately.

claims.claim: In previous models, short-circuit initiation was assumed to be co-located at the coordinates where the principal tensile stress first reaches a certain magnitude, for example 10 MPa.
claims.quote: The short-circuit initiation was assumed to be co-located at the same coordinates at which the principal tensile stress first reaches a certain magnitude (for example, 10 MPa in Ref. [7]).

claims.claim: The separator undergoes mechanical failure at a maximum through-thickness strain of 0.93, meaning the element thickness is compressed to 7% of its initial thickness.
claims.quote: when εmax = 0.93 (which means that the thickness of the element is compressed to 7% of initial thickness), the numerically predicted and the experimentally measured global failure strains match

claims.claim: A larger electrical contact area between cathode and anode active layers produces a lower short-circuit resistance, larger current, and faster voltage drop.
claims.quote: Under identical conditions, a larger eroded volume fraction indicates a larger electrical contact area between the cathode and anode active layers, which will produce a more significant short (lower resistance values, larger current and faster voltage drop).

claims.claim: Short-circuits involving very small contact areas do not result in thermal runaway because the voltage drop is so slow that heat generated is dissipated before sufficient temperature rise can cause runaway.
claims.quote: This result, when extrapolated, is useful in explaining why short-circuits involving very small contact areas do not result in thermal runaway. The voltage drop is so slow that any heat generated from such shorts is dissipated before sufficient temperature rise can result in a runaway.

claims.claim: When the electrical contact area is exceedingly large in a small-format cell, energy is released within milliseconds before current density can rise to values large enough to generate significant heat, whereas a large-format cell retains sufficient energy to generate heat leading to follow-on reactions.
claims.quote: when the contact area is exceedingly large, the energy content of the small format cell is released within milliseconds before the current density can rise to sufficiently large values to generate any heat; whereas, a large-format cell will continue to have sufficient energy available across the same time-scale, and will generate sufficient heat to result in the follow-on reactions.

claims.claim: At the instant of short-circuit, the magnitude of current density increases approximately 10^5 times compared to pre-short levels.
claims.quote: From the instance of short-circuit, the magnitude of the current density increases about 10^5 times.

claims.claim: A larger electrical contact area results in a lower maximum local temperature during short-circuit due to faster energy release.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature for a larger value of contact area is lower due to faster energy release.

claims.claim: No instantaneous rise in temperature was observed after internal short-circuit; instead, cell temperature increased gradually to a peak value and then decreased.
claims.quote: No instantaneous rise in temperature was observed after the internal short. Instead, the cell temperature increased gradually to a peak value and then started to decrease.

claims.claim: The electronic conductivity of the separator is assumed to be over six orders of magnitude smaller than that of the other cell component layers.
claims.quote: the electronic conductivity of the separator is assumed to be over six orders of magnitude smaller than that of the other layers.

claims.claim: The one-way coupling approach does not capture mechanical deformation resulting from changes to material properties such as melting of cell components during thermal or electrical propagation.
claims.quote: The limitation of this approach is that we inherently assume that the mechanical deformation resulting from changes to the material properties (e.g., melting of different cell components) is not captured in modeling the thermal/electrical propagation.

claims.claim: An optimal EG mass fraction of 15–20 wt% in paraffin/EG composite PCM is recommended for battery thermal management, because increasing EG mass fraction increases thermal conductivity but simultaneously lowers phase change enthalpy, creating a competitive trade-off.
claims.quote: a higher mass fraction of EG in the composites can increase thermal conductivity, however, it simultaneously lowers the phase change enthalpy. The numerical results show that there exists an optimal mass fraction of EG in composite PCM and the EG mass fraction of 15–20% is recommended to be used for battery thermal management.

claims.claim: For the PCM module (without PGS), a cell-to-cell spacing of up to 14 mm is required to prevent thermal runaway propagation, at which the peak temperature of adjacent cells remains below the 130 °C threshold.
claims.quote: the propagation of thermal runaway can be prevented by leaving spacing up to 14 mm in which the peak temperature is below the threshold point (130 °C).

claims.claim: The PCM/PGS module prevents thermal runaway propagation at a cell-to-cell spacing of only 2 mm (L = 2 mm), reducing composite PCM consumption by 71.4% compared to the PCM module which requires 14 mm spacing.
claims.quote: the use of PGS lowers the spacing (i.e. less composite PCM consumption) by a decreasing rate of ∼71.4% in comparison with PCM module.

claims.claim: During five dynamic discharge–charge cycles, the PCM/PGS module with a convective heat transfer coefficient of 50 W m⁻² K⁻¹ achieves comparable thermal performance to the PCM module with 200 W m⁻² K⁻¹, indicating energy savings from PGS addition.
claims.quote: the thermal performance of PCM/PGS module with a convective heat transfer coefficient of 50 W m−2 K−1 is comparable to PCM module with 200 W m−2 K−1, indicating that the use of PGS is beneficial for thermal fluctuations and energy saving.

claims.claim: When composite PCM thickness is only 2 mm per side (4 mm total cell-to-cell spacing) in the PCM module, thermal runaway triggered in cell #2 propagates to adjacent cells #1, #3, and #8, with peak temperatures in the range of 450–500 K.
claims.quote: the thermal runaway on adjacent cells (#1, #3 and #8) is triggered and then propagation occurs in the PCM module when the thickness of composite PCM is 2 mm.

claims.claim: Increasing bulk density of paraffin/EG composites from 714 kg m⁻³ to 950 kg m⁻³ at 30 wt% EG increases thermal conductivity from 11.84 W m⁻¹ K⁻¹ to 15.16 W m⁻¹ K⁻¹, an increase of approximately 28%.
claims.quote: when the density increases from 714 kg m−3 to 950 kg m−3 under the EG mass fraction of 30 wt%, the thermal conductivity will rise from 11.84 W m−1 to 15.16 W m−1, almost an increase by 28%.

claims.claim: PGS (1.5 mm thick) has an in-plane thermal conductivity of 800 W m⁻¹ K⁻¹, which is twice that of copper.
claims.quote: The PGS is 1.5 mm thick and has a thermal conductivity of 800 W m−1 in the in-plane directions, which is twice than that of copper.

claims.claim: At a convective heat transfer coefficient of 50 W m⁻² K⁻¹, the average temperature difference (ΔT) across the PCM/PGS module is approximately 2.67 K compared to 8.37 K for the PCM module during dynamic cycles, a reduction of approximately 68%.
claims.quote: the average ΔT of PCM/PGS module has a decrease rate of ∼68% (i.e. 2.67 K vs. 8.37 K) compared to PCM module during the whole cycles when the convective heat transfer coefficient is 50 W m−2 K−1.

claims.claim: In the PCM module without PGS, heat accumulation during dynamic cycling causes progressive Tmax increases across cycles, reaching 401.2 K at the end of the fifth discharge at a convective heat transfer coefficient of 10 W m⁻² K⁻¹.
claims.quote: the Tmax at the end of discharge for PCM module is 325.9 K, 353.4 K, 375.8 K, 390.9 K and 401.2 K during the first, second, third, fourth and fifth cycles, respectively

claims.claim: For the PCM module under failure conditions with L = 2 mm, the peak temperature of adjacent cell #1 is 495.9 K reached at 144 s, whereas for the PCM/PGS module with the same spacing the peak temperature of T1 is 400.6 K reached at 68 s, remaining below the thermal runaway threshold.
claims.quote: the peak temperature of T1 is much lower than that of the PCM module (400.6 K v.s. 495.9 K) and the time to reach the peak temperature is much shorter (68 s v.s. 144 s) when the L is 2 mm.

claims.claim: Under EV-ARC test conditions, the maximum internal temperature of the NCM523/graphite prismatic cell (60 Ah) reached 994.8°C, while the maximum surface temperatures (front and side) were 565.8°C and 561.7°C respectively, a difference of nearly 430°C.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature of Tf and Ts was 565.8 °C and 561.7 °C during the test, and the maximum internal temperature Tin was 994.8 °C, which was nearly 430 °C higher than the surface temperature.

claims.claim: The EV-ARC-measured TR characteristic temperatures for the 60 Ah NCM523/graphite prismatic cell were T1 (self-generated heat onset) = 72.4°C, T2 (TR trigger) = 222.7°C, and T3 (TR maximum) = 994.8°C.
claims.quote: It indicates that the characteristic temperature T1 is 72.4 °C, T2 is 222.7 °C, and T3 is 994.8 °C.

claims.claim: Below 60°C, there is almost no internal exothermic reaction in the Li-ion cell, and voltage drop is due to electrochemical properties rather than internal short circuit.
claims.quote: When the cell temperature was below 60 °C, there was almost no internal exothermic reaction; at this time, the cell was slowly warming up because of the rising temperature of the EV-ARC chamber, and the voltage drop was mainly due to the electrochemical properties of the cell rather than the internal short circuit.

claims.claim: Above 110°C, the diaphragm inside the cell gradually melts, generating a weak internal short circuit accompanied by voltage drop and increasing temperature rise rate.
claims.quote: As the temperature continued to rise, the diaphragm gradually melted, a weak internal short circuit was generated inside the cell, and the voltage dropped while the temperature rise rate continued to increase.

claims.claim: At approximately 200°C, the cell voltage drops sharply and the temperature rate reaches 1°C·s−1, marking the TR trigger temperature T2.
claims.quote: When the temperature reached about 200 °C, the cell voltage dropped sharply with the internal temperature of the cell rose rapidly. Several seconds later, the temperature rate reached 1 °C·s−1. At this moment, the temperature was T2.

claims.claim: Nail penetration does not introduce additional energy into the cell, so the TR energy under nail-penetration abuse (6.96×10⁵ J) was almost the same as that under the EV-ARC test (7.19×10⁵ J).
claims.quote: It can be seen that the TR energy of the nail-penetration abuse test was almost the same as that of the EV-ARC test, which also confirmed that the nail did not introduce extra energy into the cell.

claims.claim: Side-heating abuse introduced approximately 2% extra energy (2.00×10⁴ J) relative to the adiabatic TR energy (Ea = 7.19×10⁵ J), which caused the maximum cell temperature (1020°C) to exceed that of the EV-ARC test (994.8°C).
claims.quote: The extra energy introduced into the cell in the side-heating abuse test was approximately 2% of Ea. However, for the overcharge abuse test, the TR energy was almost 1.5 times more than Ea.

claims.claim: Overcharge abuse introduced 4.48×10⁵ J of extra electrical energy into the cell, resulting in a total TR energy of 1.17×10⁶ J, approximately 1.5 times the adiabatic TR energy Ea, and caused the internal thermocouple to fail.
claims.quote: the electric energy charged into the cell was 124.4 Wh (4.48 × 10⁵ J) collected by the charger... for the overcharge abuse test, the TR energy was almost 1.5 times more than Ea... the TR process was too aggressive and caused the failure of the internal implantation thermocouple.

claims.claim: Mass loss rate of the triggering cell (C1) under overcharge abuse reached 70.16%, compared to approximately 48.53% and 45.89% for the triggering cell under nail-penetration and side-heating abuse, respectively.
claims.quote: C1 has the highest mass loss rate in each test, even up to 70% in the overcharge test, due to the extra introduced energy.

claims.claim: At the module level, the total TRP time was shortest for overcharge abuse (146.6 s), followed by side-heating (158.5 s), and longest for nail-penetration (177.8 s), indicating that overcharge-triggered TRP releases energy fastest and is most hazardous.
claims.quote: The module had the shortest TRP time and lost the most mass in the overcharge test, which to some extent, indicates that the TRP triggered by the overcharge abuse would be more hazardous.

claims.claim: For cells C2–C4 in the module, the mass loss rate was relatively similar (~33%) regardless of the abuse method used to trigger C1, indicating that the abuse method's influence diminishes after the first two TR cells.
claims.quote: However, for C2–C4, the difference in their mass loss is not significant, and the values are basically around 33%.

claims.claim: During overcharge-triggered TRP, the pressure relief valve of C1 broke 83 seconds before TR and ejected electrolyte, which is identified as a possible reason for the relatively low surface temperature (~650°C) of C1 compared to C3 and C4 (~1000°C).
claims.quote: 83 s before C1 TR, its pressure relief valve suddenly broke and started to eject a large amount of electrolyte to the outside, which may be the reason for the low temperature of C1.

claims.claim: Under overcharge abuse, the cell was overcharged to approximately 141% SOC at the module level before TR occurred, with a maximum overcharge voltage of 12.7 V.
claims.quote: The maximum overcharge voltage was 12.7 V, after which it quickly dropped to 0. At this time, C1 was overcharged to about 141% SOC.

claims.claim: During side-heating TRP, the temperatures of cells C2–C4 exceeded that of C1 by more than 100°C due to intensifying flames propagating from the sides of those cells.
claims.quote: As the flames became more intense, they were also ejected from the side of C2-C4, which resulted in a higher temperature of C2–C4 than C1 by more than 100 °C.

claims.claim: For the overcharge abuse test at cell level, using an internal implantation thermocouple is not a reliable method for measuring internal temperature because the excessive extra energy causes thermocouple failure.
claims.quote: for the overcharge abuse test, it is difficult to measure internal temperature due to the excessive extra energy.

claims.claim: Battery self-heating begins at 70–90 °C due to decomposition of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film.
claims.quote: When the temperature rises up to 70–90 °C, the battery starts self-heating due to the decomposition of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film

claims.claim: The cathode active material decomposes and generates oxygen at elevated temperatures above approximately 250 °C, which can then oxidize the electrolyte or react with anode active material.
claims.quote: The cathode active material also decomposes and generates oxygen as the battery temperature goes higher. The released oxygen can oxidize the electrolyte or even react with the anode active material, bringing the battery temperature to a higher value.

claims.claim: Combustion of flammable gases released during thermal runaway reactions always occurs outside the battery and may cause significant damage to accessories in the battery module.
claims.quote: The combustion always occurs outside the battery and may cause significant damage on the accessories in the battery module.

claims.claim: Dahn's thermal runaway model could predict the onset of battery TR well when compared to oven tests, but failed to predict the maximum temperature because not all exothermic reactions were included.
claims.quote: Dahn's model could predict the onset of battery TR well when comparing to the oven tests, but failed in the prediction of the maximum temperature, since not all the exothermic reactions were included in the model.

claims.claim: For the Li(Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3)O2/graphite battery studied, the heat generation of sample Cat+Ele was dominated by self-decomposition of cathode active material, while the reaction between cathode active material and electrolyte produced limited heat, mainly due to solvent vaporization before oxygen release at around 250 °C.
claims.quote: That indicated that the heat generation of sample Cat+Ele was dominated by the self-decomposition of cathode active material, while the reaction between cathode active material and electrolyte produced limited heat, mainly due to the vaporization of the solvents (the boiling temperatures of DMC, EMC and EC are 91 °C, 110 °C and 248 °C, respectively) before the release of oxygen from the cathode active material at around 250 °C.

claims.claim: Battery heat generation before 250 °C is dominated by reactions on the anode/electrolyte interface, including SEI film decomposition and the reaction between anode active material and electrolyte.
claims.quote: Therefore, the battery heat generation before 250 °C was dominated by the reactions on the anode/electrolyte interface, such as the decomposition of SEI film, and the reaction between anode active material and electrolyte. The reactions in sample Cat+An began to contribute to the total battery heat generation after the temperature reached 250 °C.

claims.claim: The combustion contribution to battery temperature response is neglected in the TR model because combustion occurs outside the batteries and its energy heats the surroundings rather than the cell.
claims.quote: However, the combustion always occurs outside the batteries and has little influence on battery temperature response, as the combustion energy is not fully utilized to heat the cell, but heat the surroundings. Therefore, the contribution of combustion to the battery temperature response is neglected in the battery TR model.

claims.claim: A decline in battery temperature rate was observed experimentally at around 115 °C due to vaporization of electrolyte and subsequent rupture of the pouch bag, an effect not captured by the TR model.
claims.quote: a decline of the battery temperature rate was observed in the experiment results at around 115 °C, resulting from the vaporization of the electrolyte and subsequent rupture of pouch bag, while the predicted temperature rate profile shows no decrease as the effects of electrolyte vaporization are not taken into consideration yet.

claims.claim: The sharp experimental rise of battery temperature rate to more than 2000 °C min⁻¹ after thermal runaway is attributed to possible massive internal short circuit and violent reactions of all cell components, which are difficult to simulate from component-level investigations alone.
claims.quote: The sharp rise of experimental battery temperature rate is due to possible massive internal short circuit and the violent reactions of all the cell components in a short time, which are difficult to simulate based on the investigations of cell components, and are thus not included in the TR model yet.

claims.claim: The predicted maximum temperature of 842.73 °C in the adiabatic TR test was very close to the experimental result of 829.33 °C, indicating that reaction enthalpies derived from DSC tests agree well with total energy released during TR.
claims.quote: Furthermore, the predicted Tmax reaches 842.73 °C, very close to the experimental result (829.33 °C), indicating that the reaction enthalpies derived from the DSC tests agree well with the total energy released during the TR process.

claims.claim: Polyethylene (PE) separators have a melting point of 135 °C and polypropylene (PP) separators have a melting point of 165 °C, leading to poor thermal stability.
claims.quote: The low melting point of polyolefins (135 °C for PE and 165 °C for PP) leads to poor thermal stability of separators, seriously affecting the safety of batteries.

claims.claim: When the temperature exceeds the melting point of polyolefin membranes, the mechanical properties of separators sharply deteriorate and the separators shrink to cause contact between the cathode and anode.
claims.quote: Furthermore, when the temperature exceeds the melting point of polyolefin membranes, the mechanical properties of separators sharply deteriorate and the separators shrink to cause the contact of the cathode and the anode.

claims.claim: The thermal shrinkage of a separator is required to be less than 5% after being heated at 100 °C for 1 hour.
claims.quote: The thermal shrinkage of the separator is required to be ＜5% after heated at 100 °C for 1 h.

claims.claim: The desirable porosity of a normal separator is approximately 40–60%; low porosity increases internal resistance and reduces ionic conductivity, while too-high porosity adversely affects mechanical strength and promotes shrinkage at elevated temperatures.
claims.quote: The desirable porosity of the normal separator is about 40-60%. When the separator owns low porosity, it sucks up insufficient liquid electrolyte that increases the internal resistance of batteries and reduces the ionic conductivity, deteriorating the electrochemical performance. In contrast, the separator with too high porosity has adverse effects on the mechanical strength of separators and tends to shrink at elevated temperatures, influencing the safety of batteries.

claims.claim: The onset of thermal runaway begins with decomposition of the SEI film on the negative electrode when the temperature rises to the onset temperature of self-heat generation, exposing the lithium-embedded anode to electrolytes, followed by electrolyte reduction and decomposition on the anode surface, further raising temperature to the trigger temperature of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: When the temperature rises to the onset temperature of self-heat generation, the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film on the negative electrode firstly decomposes and results in the exposure of the lithium-embedded anode in electrolytes. Subsequently, the electrolytes are reduced and decomposed on the anode surface and the temperature further rises to reach the trigger temperature of thermal runaway.

claims.claim: At 140 °C for 30 minutes, the Al2O3-PDA dual-coated PE separator shows no obvious thermal shrinkage, whereas pure PE, Al2O3-coated PE, and PDA-coated PE separators exhibit shrinkage of 36.8%, 21.1%, and 26.3%, respectively.
claims.quote: After treating at 140 °C for 30 min, the Al2O3-PDA coated PE separator shows no obvious thermal shrinkage, whereas the pure PE, Al2O3 coated PE, and PDA coated PE separators exhibit a shrinkage of 36.8%, 21.1%, and 26.3%, respectively.

claims.claim: When heated at 150 °C for 30 minutes, the pristine PE separator showed 70% thermal shrinkage, the PE separator with a SiO2 nanoparticle (SP) coating layer had 54.1% shrinkage, and the silica tube (ST) coated separator exhibited 18.95% thermal shrinkage.
claims.quote: When heated at 150 °C for 30 min, the pristine PE separator showed 70% thermal shrinkage, and the PE separator with a SP coating layer had a thermal shrinkage of 54.1%. In contrast, the ST coated separator exhibited 18.95% thermal shrinkage.

claims.claim: The PE microsphere shutdown layer on a PP membrane can melt and block the pores of PP within 3 seconds at 110 °C and within 1 second at 120 °C.
claims.quote: The shutdown layer could melt and block off the pores of PP within 3 s at 110 °C and 1s at 120 °C.

claims.claim: In an adiabatic short circuit test, the PM/PP separator maintained cell temperature below 115 °C, while LiCoO2/Li cells with the PP/PE/PP separator reached a final temperature of 163 °C.
claims.quote: An adiabatic short circuit test showed that the PM/PP separator stably maintained the cell temperature below 115 °C, while the LiCoO2/Li cells with the PP/PE/PP separator reached the final temperature of 163 °C.

claims.claim: In a nail penetration test of a Graphite/NCM523 full cell, the maximum temperature of the cell with the self-extinguishing separator was only 37.2 °C, reduced by 74% compared to the normal cell.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature of the cell with the self-extinguishing separator was only 37.2 °C, reduced by 74% compared to the normal cell.

claims.claim: The content of nanoparticles within a polymer matrix in electrospun separators is limited by increased viscosity of the spinning dope, typically to less than 15 wt%, and at high levels particles tend to aggregate and damage membrane quality.
claims.quote: Moreover, the content of nanoparticles within the polymer matrix is limited by the increased viscosity of the spinning dope (typically <15 wt%), and at high levels, the particles tend to aggregate to damage the quality of the membrane.

claims.claim: The ZrO2 powder-modified PVDF-HFP separator loses its structural integrity within 16 seconds when exposed to fire due to decomposition of PVDF-HFP, while the ZrO2 fiber-based PVDF-HFP separator remains stable for 240 seconds.
claims.quote: When getting close to fire, the ZrO2 powder-modified PVDF-HFP separator loses its structural integrity within 16 s because of the decomposition of PVDF-HFP and the exposure of ZrO2 powder. In comparison, the ZrO2 fiber-based PVDF-HFP separator remains stable for 240 s.

claims.claim: In dendrite-detecting tri-layer separators, when lithium dendrites on the anode grow and reach the intermediate copper detection layer, the voltage between the copper layer and the anode suddenly drops from 3 V to 0 V, providing an immediate alert of an upcoming internal short circuit.
claims.quote: When the battery works normally, the voltage between the intermediate copper layer and the anode can be monitored and remained stable (3 V) owing to the electrochemical potential difference. Once the lithium dendrites on the anode grow and reach the intermediate metal layer, the voltage suddenly dropped to 0 V and can be immediately detected, alerting the upcoming internal short circuits.

claims.claim: The TPP@PVDF-HFP core-shell separator releases the flame retardant TPP into the electrolyte when the battery temperature reaches the melting point of PVDF-HFP (160 °C), extinguishing flames completely within 0.4 seconds.
claims.quote: When thermal runaway occurred and the temperature inside the battery reached the melting point of PVDF-HFP (160 °C), the polymer protective shell melted to release TPP into the electrolyte, rapidly diminishing the flames and completely extinguishing within 0.4 s.

claims.claim: 90% of safety accidents in LIBs originate from internal short circuits accompanied by increases in temperature and pressure.
claims.quote: 90% safety accidents of LIBs originate from the internal short circuits accompanied by the increase of the temperature and the pressure.

claims.claim: The internal short circuit (ISC) is regarded as the initial and milestone safety event followed by possible thermal runaway, even fire/explosions in lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: Generally, the internal short circuit (ISC) is regarded as the initial and milestone safety event followed by possible thermal runaway, even fire/explosions.

claims.claim: A major ISC is defined by a sharp voltage drop of more than the order of magnitude of 0.1 V.
claims.quote: we define such ISCs as a 'major ISC' with a shear voltage drop of more than the order of magnitude of 0.1 V.

claims.claim: A minor ISC has a voltage drop of less than 1 mV because the resistance of minor ISCs can be up to 100 Ω.
claims.quote: The voltage drop of 'minor ISCs' is small (≤1 mV) since the resistance of 'minor ISCs' can be up to 100 Ω.

claims.claim: Under compression loading of a cylindrical cell, the separator fracture leads to minor ISC rather than the coating fracture, because the coatings fracture approximately 0.2 mm earlier than the separator failure point.
claims.quote: Note that the coatings fracture a little earlier (about 0.2 mm) than Point 1, indicating that it is the fracture of the separator leading to minor ISCs rather than the coating fracture.

claims.claim: The major ISC is triggered when the separator, foil, and coating fail simultaneously, because the cracks in the foil expose its inner material so that active particles and binders can no longer shield direct contact between the copper and aluminum foil.
claims.quote: since the cracks of the foil expose its partial inner material, the active particles and binders can no longer shield the direct contact between the copper and aluminum foil such that the major ISC is triggered.

claims.claim: Under four different mechanical loading conditions (compression, indentation, three-point bending, offset compression), the PEEQ range for minor ISC criteria is 0.2119–0.2358, and for major ISC criteria is 0.3280–0.3285.
claims.quote: The range of the PEEQ is 0.2119–0.2358 for minor ISCs, while the range is 0.3280–0.3285 for major ISCs under the four loading conditions.

claims.claim: The critical strain values for minor and major ISC are SOC-dependent parameters because SOC affects the mechanical properties of each battery component.
claims.quote: the SOC will affect the critical values of minor and major ISCs (shown in Fig. S4†) due to the change of mechanical properties of each component, which means ε̄eq,minor and ε̄eq,major are SOC-dependent parameters.

claims.claim: Under indentation loading, the foil fractures earlier than the separator when the anode and separator are stacked together under compression, with foil damage initiating at the corner when true strain ε = 0.526 while the separator remains intact.
claims.quote: The computational model indicates that the damage of the foil was initiated at the corner when the true strain ε = 0.526 while the separator remained intact during Stage 2 and fractured at Point 3. It proves that the foil fractures earlier than the separator under compression when the anode and separator are stacked together.

claims.claim: Existing phenomenological stress- and strain-based ISC criteria are only valid for major ISCs where the voltage drop is unrecoverable and significant, and possess limited generality due to lack of underlying mechanism description.
claims.quote: a few phenomenological stress- and strain-based criteria were suggested but only valid for major ISCs where the voltage drop is unrecoverable and significant. Due to the lack of an underlying mechanism description for the short circuit, such criteria only possess limited generality.

claims.claim: At the minor ISC point (Point 1) under compression, the minor ISC resistance is approximately 200 Ω and the estimated temperature rise is no more than 2.12 °C, making the temperature curve rise not obvious.
claims.quote: At Point 1, the separator failed while the foil remained complete, leading to a circuit with an approximate minor ISC resistance Rminor ≈ 200 Ω, and the temperature rise at Point 1 is estimated ΔT1 ≤ 2.12 °C, which makes the temperature curve rise not obvious.

claims.claim: At the major ISC point (Point 6) under compression, the major ISC resistance is 1.5 Ω and the estimated temperature rise is 20.36 °C.
claims.quote: the simultaneous failure of the separators and electrodes leads to a circuit with a major ISC resistance Rmajor = 1.5 Ω according to the ΔU = 0.1 V determined by the definition of the major ISC. The temperature rise at Point 6 can be calculated ΔT2 = 20.36 °C.

claims.claim: Offset compression causes minor ISC more easily (at nominal strain d/R = 0.178) because the local PEEQ at the center of the jellyroll increases fast at the beginning, while indentation loading triggers major ISC more easily due to PEEQ concentration near the indenter, and bending cannot trigger a major ISC.
claims.quote: the offset compression causes the minor ISC more easily (at nominal strain d/R where d is the displacement and R is the radius of the battery of 0.178) because the local PEEQ at the center of the jellyroll increases fast at the beginning under this loading condition, while the indentation loading triggers the major ISC more easily due to the PEEQ concentration near the indenter. In bending, no major ISC can be triggered.

claims.claim: The minor ISC can be mitigated by increasing the elongation of the separator and reducing the damage area when the separator fractures.
claims.quote: The minor ISC can be mitigated by increasing the elongation of the separator and reducing the damage area when the separator fractures.

claims.claim: The cathode and separator mechanical behaviors are independent of SOC value, but the anode shows obvious SOC dependence.
claims.quote: It is discovered that the cathode and separator were independent of the SOC value; however, the anode showed obvious SOC dependence.

claims.claim: X-ray computed tomography and radiography for operando characterization of cells during mechanical deformation have a spatial resolution at the magnitude of 10 μm, and due to temporal and spatial resolution limitations it is difficult to capture separator failure during deformation before/upon ISC triggering.
claims.quote: X-ray computed tomography and radiography were developed with built-in thermal sensors to conduct the operando characterization of a cell with the resolution at the magnitude of 10 μm. Due to the temporal and spatial resolution limitations, it is also difficult to capture the separator failure during the deformation before/upon the triggering of the ISC.

claims.claim: In 5 Ah LIB cells, adding 5 wt% FPPN to standard electrolyte significantly reduces the self-heating rate in the temperature range from 80°C to 110°C, causing slower cell heating and a delayed thermal runaway compared to cells with standard electrolyte.
claims.quote: The FPPN containing cells exhibit a lower self heating in the temperature range of 80 °C and 110 °C (marked by red rectangle) that causes a slower cell heating and in consequence a delayed thermal runaway in comparison to the cells with standard electrolyte.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in the tested 5 Ah cells occurs at 218 ± 2°C, defined by a heating rate threshold above 10 K·min⁻¹ as per Doughty et al.
claims.quote: At 218 ± 2 °C the thermal runaway occurs according to the threshold value of a corresponding heating rate above 10 K·min⁻¹ as defined by Doughty et al.

claims.claim: Standard electrolyte cells exceed 3 bar internal pressure (3.1 ± 0.1 bar at 250°C) during calorimetric testing, whereas cells containing 5 wt% FPPN remain below 3 bar (2.7 ± 0.2 bar at 250°C).
claims.quote: the standard cells exceed 3 bar (3.1 ± 0.1 bar at 250 °C) whereas the safety optimized cells remain below the 3 bar pressure (2.7 ± 0.2 bar at 250 °C).

claims.claim: Nail penetration and external short circuit experiments provide no significant difference in safety performance between standard and FPPN-containing 5 Ah cells.
claims.quote: While nail penetration and external short circuit experiments provide no significant difference between standard and FPPN-containing cells, an increased overcharge tolerance and a favorable thermal stability at ≥120 °C in overcharge and oven experiments could be shown.

claims.claim: During overcharge experiments, FPPN-containing cells exhibit lower maximum voltages (5.01 V and 4.96 V) compared to standard electrolyte cells (5.25 V and 5.11 V), indicating improved overcharge tolerance.
claims.quote: Whereas cells containing standard electrolyte exhibit maximum voltages of 5.25 V and 5.11 V, cells with FPPN show lower maximum voltages of 5.01 V and 4.96 V (Figure 4a).

claims.claim: The reduction in self-heating rate and gas formation in FPPN-containing cells is attributed to the additive's ability to scavenge radical intermediate products that sustain self-heating reactions.
claims.quote: Both observations reasonably can be attributed to the additive's ability to scavenge radical intermediate products that sustain the self-heating reactions.

claims.claim: During external short circuit experiments, the energy released is too large for 5 wt% FPPN additive to produce measurable differences compared to standard electrolyte cells.
claims.quote: During the rapid discharge, an excessive amount of energy is released within a small time. The amount of energy and the accompanied heat generation is too huge to see small changes introduced by addition of 5 wt % electrolyte additive.

claims.claim: In a parallel connected 18650-type LIB module, the total TR propagation time was 83 seconds faster than in the disconnected module, with the TR propagation interval between rows 2 and 3 reduced to 8 seconds compared to 79 seconds in the disconnected module.
claims.quote: the whole TR propagation process was 83 s faster than the disconnected one... The TR propagation interval between rows 2 and 3 decreased to 8 s, which was 71 s shorter than that in the disconnected module.

claims.claim: In the parallel connected module, the minimum TR onset temperature reached 77.3 °C for battery #5, whereas batteries in the disconnected module experienced similar TR onset temperatures throughout propagation.
claims.quote: Tr significantly decreased from battery #2 and reached a minimum of 77.3 °C for battery #5 in the parallel connected module... In the LIB module without parallel connections, the batteries experienced similar Tr during the TR propagation.

claims.claim: Enhanced heat transfer through pole connectors was identified as the major cause of intensified TR propagation hazard in the parallel connected module, while charge capacity during TR was negligible (only 8.5 mAh for battery #1) and could not be the major cause.
claims.quote: the enhanced heat transfer was demonstrated to be the major cause of the intensified hazard... only 8.5 mAh electricity was transferred to battery #1 during the TR, and the influence on the TR intensity could be neglected.

claims.claim: Once the critical control temperature (Tc) falls below 100 °C (Tboil), water mist cooling efficiency is significantly weakened because the cooling process depends mainly on the sensible heat of water rather than latent heat evaporation.
claims.quote: Once Tc is below 100 °C, as represented by the imagination curve, WM cannot present efficient cooling performance owing to the relatively low sensible heat, which eventually leads to the worst control effect.

claims.claim: During TR propagation in the parallel connected module, the maximum charge current reached 25.7 A (approximately 10 C-rate) for battery #2, but the voltage variation across the module was approximately 0.1 V and the charge capacity was negligible.
claims.quote: for battery #2, the maximum charge current reached 25.7 A (approximately 10 C)... A voltage drop of approximately 0.1 V was detected during the LIB TR in the module, and the charge capacity during the TR was calculated and could be neglected during the tests.

claims.claim: Water mist with a 60-second application duration prevented TR propagation in batteries #4, #5, and #6 in both connected and disconnected modules, but TR still occurred in battery #3 of the parallel connected module during WM application at an onset temperature of 95.9 °C.
claims.quote: the WM did not present a significant cooling effect on battery #3, and TR occurred during the WM application with an onset temperature of 95.9 °C... The TR propagation process was prevented in both the modules, and batteries #4, #5, and #6 were free from TR.

claims.claim: The first abrupt temperature change for 100% SOC 18650 LIBs was 133°C at 311 seconds in Hefei (100.8 kPa) and 125°C at 282 seconds in Lhasa (64.3 kPa), indicating higher crack temperature and longer time to crack at higher ambient pressure.
claims.quote: Termerature jump and violent exothermic reactions occurr for the 100% SOC LIB when the surface temperatures reach 133°C and 125°C at 311 and 282 seconds in Hefei and Lhasa, respectively.

claims.claim: The relief valve of a LIB cracks when the pressure difference between internal and ambient pressure reaches a critical value, and lower ambient pressure reduces the required crack pressure differential, causing earlier crack timing.
claims.quote: The relief valve will crack when the pressure difference between internal and ambient pressures reaches to a critical value. This is the main reason leading to the different ignition time at two ambient pressures.

claims.claim: The differential crack pressure between Lhasa and Hefei is −36.5 kPa, calculated as POut-Lhasa minus POut-Hefei (64.3 kPa − 100.8 kPa), meaning the relief valve in Lhasa requires less internal pressure to crack.
claims.quote: dPCrack ¼ PCrack−Hefei–PCrack−Lhhasa ¼ POut − Lhasa − POut−Hefei ¼ 64:3kPa − 100:8kPa ¼ −36:5kPa

claims.claim: The average burning rate of LIBs in Hefei (100.8 kPa) is larger than in Lhasa (64.3 kPa) for all tested conditions, and although LIBs ignite earlier in Lhasa, the total mass loss time is longer than in Hefei, indicating a lower mass loss rate at lower ambient pressure.
claims.quote: Although it is found that LIBs are ignited earlier in Lhasa than in Hefei, the total mass loss time is longer than that of Hefei. In other words, the MLR of LIBs in Lhasa is lower than that of Hefei.

claims.claim: The estimated pressure effect factor n (from _m ∝ P^n) varies within the range of 0.083 to 1.39 for LIB fire depending on pack conditions, which is different from the range observed for steady pool fires.
claims.quote: The values of n for LIBs fire at different conditions are estimated as shown in Table 2. It is quite different for different conditions and varies within the range of 0.083‐1.39, which is different from the range of steady pool fire given in the literature.

claims.claim: Ambient pressure has a greater effect on heat release rate and total heat release than on mass loss, and the effective combustion heat under low ambient pressure is lower than under normal pressure conditions.
claims.quote: The ambient pressure has greater effect on the HRR and THR than the mass loss, and the effective combustion heat under the low ambient pressure is lower than that in normal pressure.

claims.claim: For 100% SOC LIBs at high pressure (Hefei), rapid ejection speed causes incomplete combustion and relatively lower total heat release (THR) compared to lower SOC batteries, while this phenomenon does not occur at low pressure (Lhasa) because batteries cannot accumulate as much combustible gas before combustion.
claims.quote: A special point should be noticed that the 100% SOC batteries in Hefei (Test A5) does not have the highest THR because of high speed ejection and incompletely combustion... However, the 100% SOC batteries in Lhasa (Test A6) do not have this phenomenon. As discussed before, there is a difference in crack pressures for batteries at two ambient pressures. So, the 100% SOC batteries in Lhasa cannot accumulate a large amount of combustible gas as that in Hefei before the combustion.

claims.claim: Effective combustion heat decreases as SOC increases for LIBs, because higher SOC results in faster ejection speeds that prevent effective combustion of ejected materials.
claims.quote: SOC has the greatest influence on the effective heat of combustion, and the horizontal 0% SOC four batteries have the biggest effective heat of combustion. However, the effective heat reduces as the SOC increases, which means the effective heat reducing instead with increasing power inside the battery. The ejection speed is particularly fast because of the accumulative energy so that the materials cannot be combusted effectively.

claims.claim: The Semenov model is suitable for chemicals with small size or low convective heat transfer coefficient, corresponding to Biot number less than 0.1.
claims.quote: Semenov model is suitable to the chemicals with small size or low convective heat transfer coefficient. According to the comparison, we select Semenov model when Bi number is less than 0.1, and Thomas model when Bi number is beyond 0.1

claims.claim: When the Biot number is less than 0.1, the results of the Thomas model become incorrect.
claims.quote: However, when the Bi number is less than 0.1, the results of Thomas model becomes incorrect.

claims.claim: The positive-solvent reaction contributes 61.4% of total heat generation during the thermal runaway period, making it the dominant reaction in battery thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the positive-solvent reaction contributes most to the TR behavior in the rate of 61.4 percentage, which indicates positive-solvent reaction plays a considerable role in battery TR.

claims.claim: In the convective heat transfer coefficient range of 5 to 40 W m-2 K-1, increasing h produces a 23 K rise in SADT with an average rise rate of 0.66 K2 m2 W-1, whereas in the range of 40 to 500 W m-2 K-1 the average rise rate drops to only 0.058 K2 m2 W-1.
claims.quote: In the range of 5 to 40 W m-2 K-1 shown in fig. 6a, the improvement to the critical ambience temperature is remarkable with 23 K of SADT rise and 0.66 K2 m2 W-1 of average rise rate. In the range of 40 to 500 W m-2 K-1 shown in fig. 6b, although the critical condition still enhances with h, the average rise rate in this range is only 0.058 K2 m2 W-1

claims.claim: Increasing cell radius from 5 to 20 mm causes approximately 14 K decrease in SADT, and increasing radius from 20 to 50 mm causes approximately 10 K further decrease in SADT for cylindrical LiCoO2/graphite cells at h=20 W m-2 K-1.
claims.quote: About 14 K and 10 K of SADT decrease when cell radius increases from 5 to 20 mm and from 20 to 50 mm, respectively.

claims.claim: The 18650 NCM/LTO battery has a higher critical threshold for thermal runaway than the 18650 LiCoO2/C battery under both low and high convective heat transfer coefficient conditions.
claims.quote: Obviously, 18650 NCM/LTO battery has higher critical threshold of TR than 18650 LiCoO2/C battery in both low and high convective heat transfer coefficient.

claims.claim: The revised Semenov and Thomas models predict the critical temperature of an 18650 LiCoO2 cell within 5 degrees of the simulated oven exposure test results.
claims.quote: These comparisons confirm the correction of the modified models. The error range of two models are both within 5 degrees.

claims.claim: Li plating is likely to happen during high C-rate charging and low-temperature charging, forming Li metal deposition on the surface of the anode.
claims.quote: Li plating is likely to happen during high C-rate charging and low-temperature charging, forming Li metal deposition on the surface of the anode [10–12].

claims.claim: The formation of Li metal during plating consumes electrolyte and Li ions, resulting in loss of lithium inventory (LLI), loss of active materials (LAM) in anodes, and impedance increment, leading to accelerated capacity fade.
claims.quote: The formation of Li metal consumes electrolyte and Li ions, resulting in loss of lithium inventory (LLI), loss of active materials (LAM) in anodes and impedance increment, leading to accelerated capacity fade eventually [13].

claims.claim: Under some extreme conditions, the separator can be penetrated by dendrite growth or mechanical abuse, directly leading to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: under some extreme conditions, the separator can be penetrated by dendrite growth or mechanical abuse, directly leading to thermal runaway.

claims.claim: 0 V (vs. Li0/Li+) is assumed to be the critical point where Li plating is severe enough to pose risks to LIBs.
claims.quote: Generally, it is assumed that 0 V (vs. Li0/Li+) is the critical point where Li plating is severe enough to pose risks to LIBs [17].

claims.claim: The reaction between Li metal and electrolyte produces 'dead lithium' and forms new solid electrolyte interface (SEI), both of which can lead to permanent capacity loss.
claims.quote: the reaction between Li metal and electrolyte not only produces 'dead lithium' but also forms new solid electrolyte interface (SEI), both of which can lead to permanent capacity loss [18].

claims.claim: Inhomogeneous Li deposition is likely to occur after high-temperature (> 45 °C) cycling.
claims.quote: Previous researches also showed that inhomogeneous Li deposition is likely to occur after high-temperature ([ 45 °C) cycling [7].

claims.claim: A nonuniform temperature distribution within the battery can make local plating of Li above 0 V (versus Li/Li+, room temperature) thermodynamically favorable.
claims.quote: Wang et al. [21] reported that a nonuniform temperature distribution within the battery can make local plating of Li above 0 V (versus Li/Li+, room temperature) thermodynamically favorable.

claims.claim: Li plating is found to be exacerbated when the cathode temperature exceeds the anode temperature.
claims.quote: Li plating is found to be exacerbated when the cathode temperature exceeds the anode temperature.

claims.claim: Low temperature conditions cause sluggish Li-ion migration, which could lead to higher overpotential and reduced nucleus radius in Li deposition.
claims.quote: low temperature conditions cause sluggish Li-ion migration, which could lead to higher overpotential and reduced nucleus radius.

claims.claim: In a study with 5% Si added in the anode, cracks can occur across the anode leading to contact loss and thickening of the SEI layer, and no detectable Li metal deposition was found by solid 7Li NMR.
claims.quote: Their study showed that even with 5% Si added in the anode, cracks can occur across the anode, leading to contact loss and thickening of the SEI layer, and no detectable Li metal deposition was found by solid 7Li nuclear magnetic resonance.

claims.claim: When applied with over 3C current, the anode potentials drop below -40 mV, leading to lithium nucleation on the anode surface.
claims.quote: their study showed that when applied with over 3C current, the anode potentials drop below - 40 mV, leading to lithium nucleation on the anode surface.

claims.claim: Differential analysis can only prove the occurrence of Li plating but cannot exclude the possibility of Li plating when no signal appears in differential curves.
claims.quote: recent study showed that Li plating may not be necessarily reflected in differential curves; therefore, this method can only prove the occurrence of Li plating but cannot exclude the possibility of Li plating [49].

claims.claim: Li plating causes extra irreversible thickening of cells beyond the approximately 10% anode expansion from delithiated to fully lithiated state during normal intercalation.
claims.quote: During the intercalation process, anode can go through about 10% expansion in volume from delithiated to fully lithiated state [52]. Li plating can cause further thickening of anodes due to the irreversible Li metal.

claims.claim: Mass spectrometry titration (MST) has a detection limit of 20 nmol Li for distinguishing inactive Li from SEI components on plated Li.
claims.quote: ex situ mass spectroscopy titration (MST) is capable of distinguishing the inactive Li from the SEI components on the plated Li with a detection limit of 20 nmol Li [62, 63].

claims.claim: The intercalation rate of deposited lithium after Li plating seems to be constant, independent of the deposited amount.
