claims.quote: they further found that the intercalation rate of deposited lithium seems to be constant, independent of the deposited amount.

claims.claim: The maximum self-heating power of Samsung 18650-33G cylindrical lithium-ion battery cells at 100% SOC was measured to be 9.95 kW, and the maximum temperature reached 889 °C during ARC testing.
claims.quote: The maximum self-heating power of commercial 18650 cylindrical lithium-ion battery cells (Samsung 18650-33G) of 100% state of charge (SOC) was measured to be 9.95 kW, and the maximum temperature reached 889 oC.

claims.claim: The TR triggering temperature T0 for Samsung 18650-33G cells was 160.6 ± 1.2 °C on average, recorded from 8 repeated ARC tests.
claims.quote: T0 was 160.6±1.2 oC on average recorded from 8 repeated tests.

claims.claim: Air as the sole interstitial material between 18650 cells cannot prevent TR propagation in either top venting or side rupture mode under the tested module energy density and battery spacing conditions.
claims.quote: under the current module energy density and battery spacing conditions, only air in the gap cannot prevent the TR propagation in any form.

claims.claim: An aluminum plate as interstitial material prevents TR propagation in top venting mode by acting as a heat sink, but fails to prevent TR propagation in side rupture mode because the energy released is fully contained within the module and saturates the heat sink.
claims.quote: the aluminum plate has the function of heat sink and heat conductor, which successfully prevents the TR propagation, but is overwhelmed in the case of side rupture. The energy released by side rupture of cell #7 is all wrapped in the module. The Al plate is saturated as heat sink.

claims.claim: Graphite composite sheet prevents TR propagation in both top venting and side rupture modes due to its high in-plane thermal conductivity (800 W m⁻¹ K⁻¹) spreading heat to the casing and its low cross-plane thermal conductivity (0.02 W m⁻¹ K⁻¹ in the barrier layer) blocking heat transfer to adjacent cells.
claims.quote: The graphite sheet has a high thermal conductivity in plane direction (800 W m-1K-1), which can spread out the heat released from the TR cell rapidly to the Al casing. On the other hand, the graphite sheet has a low thermal conductivity in the axial direction (25 W m-1K-1) due to the sandwich structure, which can effectively shelter the cells adjacent to the TR cell.

claims.claim: Side rupture of the triggering cell significantly increases the possibility of TR propagation compared to top venting, because in side rupture mode the expelled energy is contained within the module enclosure.
claims.quote: It can be seen that side rupture of the triggering cell can significantly increase the possibility of TR propagation and managing the top venting path is critical for thermal management.

claims.claim: Al extrusion as interstitial material prevents TR propagation in both top venting and side rupture modes, with maximum neighboring cell temperatures of 102 °C and 115 °C respectively, both below the TR triggering threshold of 160.6 °C.
claims.quote: The temperature of the neighboring cell 3 reached highest value of 102 oC, which did not trigger TR and the rest cells remained stable... The temperature of adjacent cell #2 reached a maximum temperature around 115 oC, and did not trigger TR.

claims.claim: In modules where TR propagation occurred, the maximum temperature of the triggered cell exceeded 1000 °C, whereas in modules where TR propagation was suppressed (e.g., graphite composite sheet, top venting), the triggered cell reached only about 800 °C.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature of the triggered cell reached about 800 oC, and is much smaller than those in modules with TR propagations, which is above 1000 oC. This is due to the large amount of energy released during TR propagations in the modules causing excessive temperature rise in the center of the modules.

claims.claim: The conclusions of this study regarding interstitial material effectiveness are stated to be more applicable to cylindrical batteries and may require adjusted structure design and material parameters for prismatic and pouch cells.
claims.quote: At present, the results of this study are more inclined to be applied to cylindrical batteries. It may also be useful for prismatic and pouch cells with higher energy, while the structure design and material parameters need to be further adjusted according to different application states.

claims.claim: Early stage internal short circuit (ISC) is difficult to detect by traditional methods such as the voltage difference method because variation of voltage is small, with less than 0.1% relative difference in voltages between the faulty submodule and any normal submodule in the same module.
claims.quote: there is less than 0.1% relative difference in voltages between the fifth submodule where the faulty cell locates and any normal submodule in the sixth module of the first cluster, according to Fig. 7 (c). Thereby, the traditional threshold fault diagnosis approaches such as the voltage difference method, are not applicable to detect the LIB faults at the early stage of ISC where variation of voltage is small.

claims.claim: Shannon entropy of voltage shows approximately 10% relative difference between the faulty submodule and any normal submodule in the same module at early stage ISC, which is much larger than the corresponding voltage difference of less than 0.1%.
claims.quote: there is about 10% relative difference in Shannon entropy of voltage between the fifth submodule where the faulty cell locates and any normal submodule in the sixth module of the first cluster, which is much larger than the difference in voltage as exhibited in Fig. 7 (c).

claims.claim: The existence of a faulty LIB cell at early stage ISC magnifies the inconsistencies among LIB cells within the same module, as indicated by a larger Δ (maximum entropy minus calculated entropy) for the module containing the faulty cell compared to all other modules.
claims.quote: the first module where the faulty cell locates has a larger Δ than any other module in the first cluster, whilst in Fig. 14 (c), the sixth module where the faulty cell locates has a larger Δ than any other module in the first cluster, indicating the existence of the faulty LIB cell magnifies the inconsistencies among the LIB cells within the same module.

claims.claim: Shannon entropy module-level inconsistency metric (Δ) for the normal case across different modules in the first cluster is less than 0.05 ppm.
claims.quote: As shown in Fig. 14 (a), for the normal case, different modules in the first cluster have similar Δ, which is less than 0.05 ppm.

claims.claim: The Shannon entropy algorithm for processing one-minute data covering temperatures, voltages, and currents for all 18,240 LIB cells has a runtime of 0.045 seconds, which is much less than one minute and satisfies the requirement for real-time fault diagnosis.
claims.quote: the runtime for the Shannon entropy algorithms for processing one-minute data is 0.045s, which is much less than one minute. Therefore, the Shannon entropy algorithms meet the requirement of real-time fault diagnosis and inconsistency evaluation for the ESS.

claims.claim: Shannon entropy calculated at module or cluster level is a lumped parameter and is not very sensitive to perturbation on parameters in a single region, though this does not prevent its use for inconsistency evaluation.
claims.quote: it should be noted that Shannon entropy calculated by Eq. (7) or Eq. (8) is a lumped parameter. Therefore, its value is not very sensitive to perturbation on parameters in a single region. However, it will not hinder the application of Shannon entropy in inconsistency evaluation so long as how much change in Shannon entropy will be caused by different values of perturbation on parameters is better understood.

claims.claim: Setting the alarm thresholds (Δthr,1, Δthr,2) higher or ρthr lower reduces the probability of false positive alarms but also makes the algorithms less sensitive, potentially leading to late or failed detection.
claims.quote: Since the data may be undermined by noise or error, setting Δthr,1 and Δthr,2 higher or ρthr lower will help reduce the probability of fault positive alarms. However, it will also make the algorithms less sensitive, which may lead to late detection or failure of detection.

claims.claim: A liquid electrolyte composed of EC, DMC, and LiPF6 exhibits conductivity greater than 10−3 S cm−1 at room temperature, but undergoes sharp reduction in conductivity at temperatures below about −10 °C due to freezing.
claims.quote: A liquid electrolyte composed of EC, DMC, and LiPF6 exhibits conductivity greater than 10−3 S cm−1 at room temperature [1–3]. A shortcoming of this system is the sharp reduction in conductivity at temperatures below about −10 ◦C, due to freezing.

claims.claim: An electrolyte composed of 1 M LiPF6 in EC, DMC and EMC (15:37:48 by weight) had a freezing point of −41 °C.
claims.quote: An electrolyte composed of 1 M LiPF6 in EC, DMC and EMC (15:37:48 by weight) had a freezing point of −41 ◦C.

claims.claim: LiPF6 undergoes exothermic runaway of the electrolyte near 175 °C as measured by ARC, whereas LiTFSI is thermally stable on storage up to 375 °C and stable up to 250 °C.
claims.quote: LiPF6: Exothermic runaway of the electrolyte near 175 ◦C (ARC) ... LiTFSI: Thermally stable on storage up to 375 ◦C; Stable up to 250 ◦C

claims.claim: The charge-transfer resistance of LiPF6-based electrolyte formulation in EC/DMC/EMC (15:37:48) at 30 °C is almost an order of magnitude higher than that of the LiTFSI-based formulation, despite similar bulk resistance.
claims.quote: Although the bulk resistance is similar in both electrolyte formulations at 30 ◦C, the charge-transfer resistance with the LiPF6-based formulation is almost an order of magnitude higher than that with LiTFSI.

claims.claim: Addition of 2 wt.% TRI to LiTFSI electrolyte delayed the onset of exothermic reaction with lithium metal from 150 °C to 160 °C, substantially reduced the maximum self-heating rate, and showed no exothermic reaction up to 230 °C.
claims.quote: With 2% TRI in LiTFSI electrolyte, the onset of the exothermic reaction was delayed to 160 ◦C. The maximum self-heating rate for electrolyte was substantially reduced by addition of TRI... The ARC experiment performed with lithium and TRI suggest that there was no exothermic reaction up to 230 ◦C.

claims.claim: The optimized electrolyte formulation 0.9 M LiTFSI in EC/DMC/EMC (15:37:48) achieved a conductivity of approximately 2 mS cm−1 at −40 °C, and adding 2 wt.% TRI did not affect this conductivity.
claims.quote: The electrolyte with 0.9 M LiTFSI had the highest conductivity, 2 mS cm−1 at −40 ◦C... The conductivity of the electrolyte was not affected by the addition of 2 wt.% TRI.

claims.claim: At −40 °C, the lithium cell with the new LiTFSI-based electrolyte could deliver almost 20% of the capacity obtained at 25 °C.
claims.quote: It was observed that, at −40 ◦C, the lithium cell could deliver almost 20% of the capacity obtained at 25 ◦C, indicating that the cell with the new electrolyte could operate down to −40 ◦C.

claims.claim: The quaternary electrolyte 0.9 M LiTFSI in EC–DMC–EMC–PC (15:37:38:10) showed lower conductivity at low temperatures (0.85 mS cm−1 at −40 °C) compared to the ternary formulation, possibly due to phase separation of EC.
claims.quote: at low temperatures the conductivity is lower (0.85 mS cm−1 at −40 ◦C and 2.12 mS cm−1 at −30 ◦C), perhaps because of phase separation of EC.

claims.claim: DSC measurements of fully charged LiNi0.8Co0.2O2 cathode with 2 wt.% TRI showed the exothermic reaction at 224 °C was delayed by 5 °C and maximum heat flow for major exotherms was lowered compared to cells without TRI additive.
claims.quote: The maximum heat ﬂow for the major exotherms is lowered with addition of TRI. The onset temperature for the exothermic reactions is shifted higher... The exothermic reaction at 224 ◦C is delayed by 5 ◦C.

claims.claim: The fully charged state of LiNi0.8Co0.2O2 cathode poses greater thermal hazards than any other state of charge.
claims.quote: The fully charged state was chosen since this state poses greater thermal hazards than any other state of charge.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway (TR) is defined as a self-sustaining, uncontrollable increase in temperature within the battery, leading to a cascade of chemical reactions and the release of large amounts of heat and gas, which can result in fires, explosions, and other hazardous situations within few minutes.
claims.quote: TR refers to a self-sustaining, uncontrollable increase in temperature within the battery, leading to a cascade of chemical reactions and the release of large amounts of heat and gas. This can ultimately result in fires, explosions, and other hazardous situations within few minutes.

claims.claim: T1 is defined as the temperature at which detectable self-heating of the battery begins due to solid electrolyte interface (SEI) decomposition.
claims.quote: T1 signifies the temperature at which detectable self-heating of the battery begins due to solid electrolyte interface (SEI) decomposition.

claims.claim: T2 represents the temperature at which TR is triggered, often associated with separator failure leading to an internal short circuit (ISC), at which point the temperature transitions from a stable level to a quasi-exponential increase.
claims.quote: T2 represents the temperature at which TR is triggered, often associated with separator failure leading to an internal short circuit (ISC). At T2, the temperature transitions from a stable level to a quasi-exponential increase.

claims.claim: T3 designates the peak temperature of TR and is identifiable as the highest point on the temperature profile.
claims.quote: T3 designates the peak temperature of TR and is identifiable as the highest point on the temperature profile.

claims.claim: The TR progression of a lithium-ion battery typically encompasses six distinct reaction phases: decomposition of the SEI membrane, reactions involving the electrolyte and active cathode material, melting of the separator, decomposition of the anode and the electrolyte, and reactions between the binder and active cathode material.
claims.quote: Illustrated in Fig. 1. is the TR progression of a battery, which typically encompasses six distinct reaction phases: commencement with the decomposition of the solid electrolyte phase interface (SEI) membrane, succeeded by reactions involving the electrolyte and active cathode material, subsequent melting of the separator, decomposition of the anode and the electrolyte, and ultimately the reactions between the binder and active cathode material.

claims.claim: Beyond 80°C, the breakdown of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer becomes evident in lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: Beyond 80 ◦C, the breakdown of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer becomes evident.

claims.claim: As the temperature of a lithium-ion battery approaches 90°C, the commencement of SEI decomposition initiates the liberation of significant gases and heat including O2, CO2, and CH4.
claims.quote: As the temperature approaches 90 ◦C, the commencement of SEI decomposition initiates the liberation of significant gases and heat (O2, CO2 and, CH4).

claims.claim: At approximately 120°C, SEI decomposition on the anode surface exposes the active material, causing lithium within the anode to react with the electrolyte and generating hydrocarbons.
claims.quote: Upon reaching approximately 120 ◦C, the SEI decomposition on the anode surface exposes the active material, causing the previously safeguarded lithium within the anode to react with the electrolyte, generating some hydrocarbons.

claims.claim: Polyethylene (PE) and polypropylene (PP) based conventional polymer separators melt at around 130°C and 170°C respectively, with separator melting leading to internal short circuit and rapid battery temperature elevation.
claims.quote: Upon reaching the melting point of the separator, typically causing the polyethylene (PE) and polypropylene (PP) based conventional polymer separators to melt at around 130 ◦C and 170 ◦C respectively. The thermal degradation of the separator further leads to the battery's internal short circuit, while simultaneously liberating a substantial amount of energy that rapidly elevates the battery's temperature.

claims.claim: At approximately 200°C, cathode decomposition undergoes rapid acceleration accompanied by significant heat release and O2 gas in lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: Upon reaching approximately 200 ◦C, cathode decomposition undergoes a rapid acceleration, accompanied by a lot of heat release and O2 gas.

claims.claim: Decomposition of electrolyte salts such as LiPF6 experiences significant acceleration when temperatures surpass 150°C, and the resulting PF5 expedites electrolyte decomposition and reacts with H2O to emit toxic HF gas.
claims.quote: The decomposition of electrolyte salts, (for example LiPF6) experiences significant acceleration when temperatures surpass 150 ◦C. The resulting PF5 expedites electrolyte decomposition and reacts with H2O to emit toxic HF gas.

claims.claim: At 235°C, the binder poly(vinylidene fluoride) reacts with Li, producing H2 gas and LiF.
claims.quote: Once temperatures reach 235 ◦C, the binder (for example poly(vinylidene fluoride) reacts with Li, producing H2 gas and LiF.

claims.claim: Around 220°C, both the rate of heat release and gas production attain their peaks during lithium-ion battery thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Around 220 ◦C, both the rate of heat release and gas production attain their peaks.

claims.claim: Mechanical abuse such as nail penetration, crushing, or dropping can result in separator tearing; electrical abuse from overcharging, over-discharging, or external short circuits can cause the separator to be pierced; and thermal abuse from overheating, fire, or high ambient temperature can lead to separator collapse—all of which trigger an internal short circuit.
claims.quote: Mechanical abuse, such as nail penetrations, crushing, or dropping the battery, can result in separator tearing. Similarly, electrical abuse from overcharging, over discharging, or external short circuits (ESC) can cause the separator to be pierced. Thermal abuse, caused by overheating, fire, or high ambient temperature, can lead to separator collapse. Regardless of how the separator is damaged, an ISC is triggered in the lithium-ion battery, causing excessive heat and intensifying electrochemical side reactions.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway TR Number (TRN) threshold is defined such that TRN > 1 signifies the onset of TR while TRN < 1 means the cell remains immune to TR.
claims.quote: The researchers also introduced dimensionless parameters referred to as the TR Number (TRN) to forecast the likelihood of TR. When TRN > 1 signifies the onset of TR while TRN < 1, the cell remains immune to TR.

claims.claim: In a study of 51 prismatic and pouch lithium-ion batteries, higher energy densities correlated with an earlier onset of TR and greater severity of TR.
claims.quote: The experimental results of TR revealed a correlation between higher energy densities and an earlier onset of TR. Additionally, the severity of the TR escalated with increased energy density within the batteries.

claims.claim: The average gas-to-capacity ratio during thermal runaway across 51 tested lithium-ion batteries was 1.96 L/Ah.
claims.quote: This analysis yielded an average gas-to-capacity ratio of 1.96 L/Ah.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of lithium-ion batteries, CO, CO2, and H2 exhibited the highest average concentrations in vented gas at 36.56%, 22.38%, and 22.27% respectively, collectively with five other components accounting for over 99% of total gas volume.
claims.quote: Notably, CO, CO2, and H2 exhibited the highest average concentrations, at 36.56 %, 22.38 %, and 22.27 %, respectively.

claims.claim: A gas sensor was capable of detecting a fault signal approximately 7 to 8 minutes prior to the actual onset of TR, providing approximately 2 minutes lead time ahead of the voltage sensor and 7 minutes ahead of the temperature sensor.
claims.quote: The analysis reveals that the gas sensor is capable of detecting the fault signal approximately 7 to 8 min prior to the actual onset of TR, which occurs around the 10-minute mark. This leads to a detection lead time of about 2 min ahead of the voltage sensor and 7 min ahead of the temperature sensor.

claims.claim: A SnO2-based ceramic semiconductor gas sensor can detect TR events earlier than a Figaro gas sensor because during the TR process, the emission of H2 and CO lags behind the release of DEC vapors, and the Figaro sensor can only detect H2 and CO.
claims.quote: This mechanism possesses the unique ability to detect TR occurrences even earlier than the Figaro gas sensor due to the fact that during the TR process, the emission of H2 and CO lags behind the release of DEC vapors and also Figaro gas sensor can only detect the former two gases.

claims.claim: The Figaro gas sensor (TGS822TF) activates an alarm signal upon detection of H2 and CO concentrations surpassing 120 ppm, with a measurement range spanning from 100 ppm to 1000 ppm at room temperature.
claims.quote: It's measurement range spans from 100 ppm to 1000 ppm at room temperature. Upon the detection of H2 and CO concentrations surpassing 120 ppm, the sensor will activate an alarm signal.

claims.claim: CO2 constitutes the primary gas component during the initial stages of lithium-ion cell TR and primarily originates from the decomposition of SEI.
claims.quote: They observed that CO2 constitutes the primary gas component during the initial stages of lithium-ion cell TR, aligning with the findings of Lammer and colleagues. The study revealed that CO2 primarily originates from the decomposition of SEI.

claims.claim: A CO2 gas sensor detected the onset of a TR event within 85 seconds, a time interval notably shorter than the propagation critical time (tcrit = 710 s) which represents the gap between the TR of the first and second cell in a cell pack.
claims.quote: Experimental outcomes demonstrated that a CO2 gas sensor detected the onset of a TR event within 85 s, a time interval notably shorter than the propagation critical time (tcrit = 710 s). The tcrit signifies the gap between the TR of the first and the second cell.

claims.claim: During TR induced by overcharging of LFP cells, H2, CO2, and CO concentration monitoring serves as primary indicators for first-level TR warnings, while HF and HCl concentrations serve as secondary indicators when the first-level warning fails.
claims.quote: Consequently, H2, CO2, and CO concentration monitoring could serve as primary indicators for first-level TR warnings for LFP cells, while monitoring HF and HCl concentrations could serve as secondary indicators when the first-level warning fails to accurately detect TR.

claims.claim: During the initial phase of TR, the force signal experiences a sudden surge due to cell expansion prior to gas release, and the force signal diminishes as the gas is released.
claims.quote: Cai et al. also highlighted that during the initial phase of TR, the force signal experiences a sudden surge due to cell expansion prior to gas release and the force signal diminishes, as the gas is released.

claims.claim: The early warning strategy for TR based on gas concentration monitoring has a limitation in that the interval between gas release and the actual onset of TR is relatively short, resulting in insufficient lead time for warnings.
claims.quote: However, a limitation lies in the relatively short interval between gas release and the actual onset of TR, resulting in insufficient lead time for warnings.

claims.claim: Batteries with higher state of charge (SOC) exhibit a tendency for rapid temperature surge to occur sooner and manifest increased internal pressures compared to batteries with lower SOC levels, amplifying the potential for battery sidewall breaching and explosion.
claims.quote: Notably, batteries with higher state of charge (SOC) exhibit a tendency for the rapid temperature surge to occur sooner compared to batteries with lower SOC levels. Furthermore, batteries at higher SOC levels manifest an inclination toward increased internal pressures, thereby amplifying the potential for battery sidewall breaching and also the possibility of explosion.

claims.claim: Once the temperature of a lithium-ion battery surpasses 250°C, heat production transitions from the anode/electrolyte interface to stem from the cathode's active material decomposition reaction coupled with reactions induced by released oxygen.
claims.quote: Initially, heat generation within the battery hinges upon the exothermic reaction occurring at the anode/electrolyte interface. Once the temperature surpasses 250 ◦C, heat production transitions to stem from the cathode's active material decomposition reaction, coupled with reactions induced by released oxygen.

claims.claim: In TR spurred by local heating in LFP batteries, the heat arising from electrolyte-anode reactions constituted 63.8% of the overall heat, whereas ISC-related heat generation remained minimal.
claims.quote: Zhang et al. determined that the heat arising from electrolyte-anode reactions constituted 63.8 % of the overall heat, whereas ISC-related heat generation remained minimal which is consistent with Ren et al.'s observations.

claims.claim: The electrochemical impedance of lithium-ion battery cells decreases as internal temperature rises, and this impedance remains relatively constant despite fluctuations in SOC, predominantly due to ionic conduction across the SEI.
claims.quote: Specifically, the impedance of cells exhibits a decrease as the internal temperature rises. Remarkably, this impedance remains relatively constant despite fluctuations in the SOCs. This stability predominantly arises from the ionic conduction across the SEI.

claims.claim: SOC plays a significant role in influencing temperature estimation through impedance analysis particularly at lower frequencies, while its effect diminishes at higher frequencies.
claims.quote: The findings showed that SOC plays a significant role in influencing temperature estimation through impedance analysis, particularly at lower frequencies, while its effect diminishes at higher frequencies.

claims.claim: A critical threshold for internal battery temperature exists at approximately 100°C, where the anode/SEI layer becomes susceptible to vulnerability, potentially leading to TR.
claims.quote: A crucial threshold or critical point for internal temperature exists at approximately 100 ◦C, where the anode/SEI layer becomes susceptible to vulnerability, potentially leading to TR.

claims.claim: A resistance-based monitoring technology for TR, using resistance and its change rate supported by thermodynamic calculations, can provide an early warning signal predicting TR over 25 minutes in advance.
claims.quote: This technology offers an early warning signal, predicting TR over 25 min in advance.

claims.claim: The resistance system (R1, R2, R3, R4, Rmax) demonstrated greater precision and efficiency in predicting and issuing early warnings for TR compared to the widely employed temperature system (T1, T2, T3).
claims.quote: In comparison with the widely employed temperature system (T1, T2, and T3), the resistance system demonstrated greater precision and efficiency in predicting and issuing early warnings for TR.

claims.claim: TR prediction models based on electrochemical mechanisms are specifically applicable for foreseeing TR triggered by mishandling or abuse, and they are not directly applicable for early detection of spontaneous TR due to unknown factors during regular battery operation.
claims.quote: Nevertheless, these aforementioned models are specifically applicable for foreseeing TR triggered by mishandling or abuse, and they are not directly applicable for early detection of spontaneous TR due to unknown factors that may arise during regular battery operation.

claims.claim: There is presently no reliable algorithm for forecasting TR using real-world vehicle data, highlighting a significant disparity between idealized experimental TR datasets and actual-world vehicle data.
claims.quote: Regrettably, there is presently no reliable algorithm for forecasting TR using real-world vehicle data. This highlights the need for further research and development in this area to bridge the existing gap.

claims.claim: The intercept frequency f0 of electrochemical impedance, defined as the point where voltage and current are in phase, is related only to the internal temperature and remains independent of the SOC of lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: The key insight lies in the fact that this intercept frequency of impedance is only related to the internal temperature and remains independent of the SOC of LIBs.

claims.claim: Surface temperature monitoring using thermocouples for anticipating TR is subject to specific time delays due to heat conduction effects.
claims.quote: Due to heat conduction effects, conventional approaches of anticipating TR through surface temperature monitoring using thermocouples are subject to specific time delays.

claims.claim: The gas sensor approach outperformed other sensor-based methods (temperature, voltage, pressure, smoke, and creep distance sensors) in detecting TR occurrences at an earlier stage, regardless of the inducing abuse mechanism.
claims.quote: The outcome of the experiment revealed that the gas sensor approach outperformed other sensor-based methods in detecting TR occurrences at an earlier stage, regardless of the inducing abuse mechanism.

claims.claim: Battery manufacturing defects can cause internal short circuits leading to TR, and the presence of impurities or metallic particles in the battery can act as catalysts for unwanted reactions, increasing the risk of TR.
claims.quote: Battery manufacturing defects, can also cause ISC, leading to TR and presence of impurities or metallic particles in the battery can act as catalysts for unwanted reactions, increasing the risk of TR.

claims.claim: Certain cathode materials exhibit instability when the temperature reaches above 125°C and are fully decomposed beyond 150°C.
claims.quote: Certain cathode materials exhibit instability when the temperature reaches above 125 ◦C, and fully decomposed beyond 150 ◦C.

claims.claim: The thickening of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) film acts as the main feature of the cell aging process, and aging causes substantial changes in capacity and internal resistance.
claims.quote: In practical scenarios, the majority of cells in use undergo aging over a period of time, consequently, their capacity and internal resistance exhibit substantial changes. Various researchers have delved into understanding cell aging mechanisms, with the thickening of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) film acts as main feature of this aging process.

claims.claim: Three-dimensional thermal abuse models for Li-ion batteries that account for SEI layer thickening can enable temperature rise predictions for both new and aged cells during thermal abuse, but fail in instances of non-uniform heating or ISC-induced TR.
claims.quote: The impact of aging on overall cell performance was factored in, enabling temperature rise predictions for both new and aged cells during thermal abuse. However, it's important to note that in instances of non-uniform heating or ISC-induced TR, the aforementioned model's fail.

claims.claim: In a parallel battery module configuration, when a short circuit occurs in the cell undergoing thermal runaway, the other normal cells transfer electric current to the cell in TR, which behaves like a load, generating Joule heating.
claims.quote: During Period I, Cell 1 was triggered into TR and a short circuit occurred in Cell 1, as shown in Fig. 9b. The other three normal cells (unaffected cells) transferred electric current to the Cell 1, which behaves like a load.

claims.claim: In a parallel battery module, the temperature of cells undergoing TR propagation (Cells 2, 3, and 4) exceeded 900°C, while the triggering cell (Cell 1) only reached 700°C.
claims.quote: the middle surface temperature of Cell 1 (as shown by TC2) reached 700°C after TR, while the temperatures of Cell 2, 3, and 4 (as shown by TC3, TC4 and TC5, respectively) exceeded 900°C.

claims.claim: The electricity transferred from normal cells to the abused cell in a parallel module leads to approximately 10°C higher temperature in the cell in TR compared to a series module configuration.
claims.quote: The result indicates that the temperature of the module in parallel during TR propagation is 10°C higher compared to the module in series.

claims.claim: The energy released during TR by Cells 1, 2, and 3 in the parallel module exceeded 100% of their initial capacity due to electricity transferred from adjacent cells, whereas Cell 4 released only 91.93% (Exp. No. 1) because it was at the lowest SOC.
claims.quote: From Table 8, it can be seen that the energy released by Cell 1, 2, and 3 exceeded 100% of the capacity. This shows that the TR propagation of the battery module in parallel is more aggressive and risky than that of the battery module in series.

claims.claim: The propagation time of TR in Cell 1 was the shortest because Cell 1 was heated by the heater with large continuous power and also influenced by Cells 2, 3, and 4 acting as a load; Cell 4 had the longest propagation time because it was at the lowest SOC during TR.
claims.quote: The propagation time of Cell 1 is the shortest. This indicates that the TR of Cell 1 was the most severe among other cells. The reason is that Cell 1 was heated by the heater with large and continuous power, and also was influenced by Cell 2, 3, and 4 as a load. The propagation time of Cell 4 is the longest, because Cell 4 was at the lowest SOC during TR with lowest energy.

claims.claim: In the simulation of an 18-cell parallel module, the minimum SOC of normal cells remained above 88% at the end of TR propagation, indicating that reduced SOC alone does not stop TR propagation.
claims.quote: The minimum SOC of normal cells is still higher than 88% at the end of the simulation. This indicates that although the SOC of normal cells decreased during the propagation, the TR propagation does not stop.

claims.claim: The temperature rise of normal cells due to Joule heat generated in their internal resistance during TR propagation in a parallel module is less than 2°C.
claims.quote: In Fig. 21(b), the temperature rise of normal cells due to Joule heat generated by internal resistance is less than 2°C.

claims.claim: When the temperature of a lithium-ion battery rises above 90°C, a series of exothermic electrochemical reactions and chemical decompositions takes place and thermal runaway occurs.
claims.quote: When the temperature rises to above 90 °C, a series of exothermic electrochemical reactions and chemical decompositions takes place, and thermal runaway occurs.

claims.claim: In nail penetration tests on coin cells, addition of approximately 4 wt. % pentadecane reduced the maximum temperature increase by approximately 60%, from approximately 75°C in the reference cell to approximately 30°C.
claims.quote: The addition of pentadecane reduces DTmax by ~60%, from ~75 °C in the reference cell to ~30 °C.

claims.claim: In impact tests on pouch cells, approximately 5 wt. % pentadecane reduced the maximum temperature increase by nearly an order of magnitude, with the TRR-modified cell showing a maximum temperature increase of only 5°C.
claims.quote: The maximum increase in temperature of the pouch cell embedded with TRR is only 5 °C, order-of-magnitude lower than that of the reference cell.

claims.claim: Addition of 4 wt. % pentadecane increases both series resistance and charge transfer resistance by approximately 50 times, with charge transfer resistance increasing from 375.2 Ω to 20004.3 Ω.
claims.quote: the charge transfer resistance (Rct) for reference and pentadecane-modified cells are 375.2 Ω and 20004.3 Ω, respectively. Both Rs and Rct increase by ~50 times with the addition of pentadecane.

claims.claim: Octane has lower thermal-runaway mitigation efficiency than pentadecane, possibly because its low boiling point of approximately 125°C causes it to evaporate after nail penetration as cell temperature rapidly increases.
claims.quote: Octane has a lower efficiency, possibly due to its low boiling point ~125 °C. After nail penetration, the temperature inside the LIB cell increases rapidly and causes evaporation of octane.

claims.claim: Icosane, which has a melting point of 36°C, can melt and flow to wet the separator after nail penetration but has higher viscosity and shorter diffusion distance than pentadecane, making it less effective as a TRR.
claims.quote: Icosane has a melting point of 36 °C and would be melted inside the cell after nail penetration. It could flow and wet the separator after melting, but with a higher viscosity and a shorter diffusion distance.

claims.claim: Under compression at 1 kPa, pentadecane travels approximately 8.7 mm on the separator in 15 seconds in an electrode layer stack, giving a diffusion rate of approximately 580 μm/s.
claims.quote: When the electrode stack is compressed at 1 kPa, pentadecane could travel 8.7 mm on separator in 15 s.

claims.claim: The diffusion distance measurement of pentadecane in the electrode stack is conservative because nail penetration or impact damage creates a loosely packed structure that favors capillary flow.
claims.quote: Such a measurement is conservative, since in a LIB cell, nail penetration or impact would damage local electrodes, leading to a loosely packed structure that favors capillary flow.

claims.claim: Paraffin wax has an appropriate melting point of approximately 45 °C and a sizable latent heat of approximately 240 J g−1, making it suitable as a heat-absorbing material in PCM-based separators.
claims.quote: Paraffin wax (PW) is chosen as the heat-absorbing material because of its appropriate melting point (≈45 °C) and sizable latent heat (≈240 J g−1).

claims.claim: The latent heat of the PW@PAN membrane is tunable from 11.74 to 135.3 J g−1 by varying the PW encapsulation ratio from 5.1% to 61.8% as PW content increases from 20% to 100%.
claims.quote: the latent heat of the PW@PAN membrane ranged from 11.74 to 135.3 J g−1 with an encapsulation ratio from 5.1% to 61.8% as the PW content increases from 20% to 100%.

claims.claim: After nail penetration, a cell with the PCM-based separator cooled down to room temperature within 35 s, whereas a cell with the Celgard separator remained at elevated temperature for 6 minutes.
claims.quote: the cell with the phase-change separator promptly cooled down to room temperature within 35 s under the abuse conditions (nail penetration), whereas the cell with the Celgard separator stays at the enhanced temperature for 6 min.

claims.claim: During nail penetration of a fully charged pouch cell, the surface temperature of the cell with the PCM-based separator was suppressed at 34.4 °C, which is 5.2 °C lower than the 39.6 °C reached by the cell with the Celgard separator.
claims.quote: the surface temperature of the routine cell using the Celgard separator immediately underwent a large temperature rise to 39.6 °C upon the occurrence of an internal short. In contrast, the surface temperature of the cell with the PCM-based separator was suppressed at 34.4 °C, which is 5.2 °C lower than that with the Celgard separator.

claims.claim: A separator with high thermal conductivity only promotes homogeneous heat distribution and lowers localized heat, but cannot dissipate total battery heat in time, so the temperature may still rapidly rise to cause safety issues.
claims.quote: the separator with high thermal conductivity only promoted a homogenous heat distribution and lowered the localized heat. The total heat in batteries cannot be dissipated in time, and eventually, the temperature may rapidly rise to cause safety issues.

claims.claim: The total heat absorbed by the PW@PAN separator during nail penetration was calculated to be 3.9 J, which is in the same order of magnitude as the approximately 0.65 J of heat generated from LiFePO4 decomposition with electrolyte during an internal short-circuit.
claims.quote: The total absorbed heat of the separator was calculated to be 3.9 J. Therefore, the total absorbed heat based on the thermal storage of the PCM was basically in the same order of magnitude as the heat generated from the decomposition of LiFePO4 with the electrolyte during the internal short-circuit process.

claims.claim: The apparent specific heat capacity of the PW@PAN separator at 44 °C is 2.76 J g−1 K−1, which is higher than that of the Celgard separator (1.97 J g−1 K−1) at the same temperature.
claims.quote: the apparent specific heat capacity was 2.76 J g−1 K−1 at 44 °C, which is higher than that of the Celgard separator (1.97 J g−1 K−1).

claims.claim: The Celgard polypropylene separator begins to thermally shrink and crinkle after thermal treatment at 120 °C for 30 minutes, whereas the PW@PAN membrane exhibits negligible change in color and dimension even up to 200 °C.
claims.quote: the PW@PAN membrane exhibits a negligible change in color and dimension even up to 200 °C. In contrast, the Celgard separator begins to thermally shrink and crinkle after thermal treatment at 120°C for 30 min.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature during the LIB thermal runaway process was 1262°C, while the maximum temperature during the ICE combustion process was 1829°C, making the ICE maximum temperature 1.4 times that of the LIB thermal runaway process.
claims.quote: The maximum temperatures were 1262 °C and 1829 °C for the LIB thermal runaway process and the ICE combustion process, respectively. The maximum temperature of the latter was 1.4 times that of the former.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rise rate (dT/dt)max during the LIB thermal runaway process was 15,660°C/min, while during the ICE combustion process it was 43,383,762°C/min, making the ICE rate 2,770 times that of the LIB process.
claims.quote: In the current study, (dT/dt)max were 15,660 °C / min during the LIB thermal runaway process and 43,383,762 °C / min during the ICE combustion process, respectively. The latter was 2,770 times that of the former.

claims.claim: The anodic SEI has higher reactivity and begins exothermic reactions in the electrolyte at approximately 90–120°C, earlier than other cell materials, followed by lithiated graphite anode and electrolyte reactions, and finally cathode material decomposition with oxygen generation.
claims.quote: anodic SEI has higher reactivity and involves in physical and chemical reactions exothermally in the electrolyte earlier than other material of the cell at about 90–120 °C, followed by the lithiated (charged) graphite anode and electrolyte reacting exothermally, and finally cathode material decomposing with generation of oxygen which reacted with the electrolyte exothermally.

claims.claim: The decomposition temperature of cathode materials increases in the order LCO < NCA < NCM < LMO < LFP, with higher decomposition temperature corresponding to lower reactivity and higher stability.
claims.quote: the decomposition temperature of the cathode material was increasing in the following order: LCO < NCA < NCM < LMO < LFP. Therefore, it is believed that the higher decomposition temperature, the lower reactivity of the cathode materials with higher stability.

claims.claim: LFP cathode material decomposition occurs without oxygen release, which is identified as one of the most important reasons why LFP batteries are safer than other chemistries, because the absence of oxygen prevents increased reactivity of other materials inside the cell.
claims.quote: The LFP decomposition reaction takes place without oxygen release. So one of the most important reasons that LFP battery is safer than others is LFP cathode material will not make the reactivity of other material inside the cell higher.

claims.claim: Tsa is defined as the lowest temperature at which automatic and continuous accelerated reactions can continue when the abuse is stopped, and at Tsa the cell heat generation rate equals the heat dissipation rate.
claims.quote: the lowest temperature at which the automatic and continuous accelerated reactions can continue when the abuse is stopped is called the thermal runaway self-accelerated chemical reaction temperature (Tsa) in this study... the cell heat generation rate is equal to the heat dissipation rate at Tsa

claims.claim: When cell temperature exceeds Tsa after abuse is stopped, the cell self-heating rate exceeds the heat dissipation rate, causing heat accumulation and automatically accelerating chemical reactions leading to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: When the cell temperature is higher than Tsa after the abuse is stopped, the cell self-heating rate is higher that the heat dissipation rate. Therefore, the cell heat can be accumulated, and the chemical reactions can be automatically accelerated, with increasing cell temperature. This will cause thermal runaway

claims.claim: External thermal management can extend the slow reaction period of LIB thermal runaway by lowering internal cell temperature, but cannot control the rapid reaction period due to limitations in internal temperature control.
claims.quote: This method can extend the slow reaction period by lowering the internal temperature of the cell, but it cannot control the rapid reaction period. This is because the internal temperature control is limited.

claims.claim: To prevent LIB re-ignition after a cell catches fire, the cell temperature must be lowered to below Tsa.
claims.quote: After the cell catches fire, its temperature should be lowered to Tsa to prevent it from re-igniting.

claims.claim: The lower the SOC, the lower the reactivity of cathode materials, because Li-ions stabilize the cathode material structure and a stability decrease is observed with removal of Li from layered oxides as SOC rises.
claims.quote: Li-ions stabilize the cathode material structure, a stability decrease was observed with the removal of Li from the layered oxides, that was, with rising state-of-charge (SOC). Therefore, it is believed that the lower SOC, the lower reactivity of the cathode materials with higher stability.

claims.claim: The thermal stability of cells after low-temperature cycling is worse than that of fresh cells.
claims.quote: The thermal stability of the cells after low-temperature cycling is worse than that of the fresh cells and less than that of the cells that are treated by high-temperature exposure.

claims.claim: Cells aged by high-temperature exposure exhibit better thermal stability than cells aged by low-temperature cycling, even when their capacity retention rates are similar.
claims.quote: Although the capacity retention rates of the cells aged by high-temperature exposure and low-temperature cycling can be similar, their thermal stabilities are quite different.

claims.claim: Consumption of active lithium at the anode surface to generate a new SEI layer results in better thermal stability in cells treated by high-temperature exposure.
claims.quote: The consumption of active lithium at the anode surface to generate a new SEI layer will result in a better thermal stability in the cells that are treated by high-temperature exposure.

claims.claim: Lithium deposition on the surface of the anode causes the thermal stability of the lithium-ion battery to become worse.
claims.quote: if there is lithium deposition on the surface of the anode, the thermal stability of the lithium-ion battery will become worse.

claims.claim: The influence of aging paths on the safety of lithium-ion batteries remains unclear, leaving uncertainties about safe operation throughout their full life cycle.
claims.quote: the influence of aging paths on the safety of the lithium-ion batteries remains unclear, leaving uncertainties about safe operation throughout their full life cycle.

claims.claim: In a confined space, the maximum temperature of the cell experiencing thermal runaway was lower than that in a semi-confined space, due to insufficient oxygen reducing combustion efficiency of ejected gases.
claims.quote: the maximum temperatures of cells 1 to 4 in the confined space (tests No. 1 to No. 3) were generally lower than those in the semi-confined space (tests No. 4 to No. 6), possibly due to insufficient oxygen.

claims.claim: The main heat transfer mode between two neighboring cells was conduction via air before one cell underwent thermal runaway, accounting for 50–83.8% of total heat transferred, and then changed to radiation when the neighboring cell underwent TR.
claims.quote: the heat was mainly transferred through conduction via the air between two neighbouring cells, in a proportion of approximately 50–83.8%. Then, the main heat transfer mode changed to radiation when the neighbouring cell underwent TR.

claims.claim: When aluminum foil was wrapped on the surface of cell 2 to decrease radiation heat absorbed, the main heat transfer mode remained conduction and did not change to radiation even when the neighboring cell underwent thermal runaway.
claims.quote: when one cell was wrapped with AF on its surface to decrease the radiation heat absorbed from the neighbouring cell, the main heat transfer mode was conduction, and it did not change even though the neighbouring cell underwent TR.

claims.claim: TR propagation speed decreased from 7.84×10⁻³ s⁻¹ to 6.14×10⁻³ s⁻¹ (cell 3 to cell 4) and from 7.84×10⁻³ s⁻¹ to 5.1×10⁻³ s⁻¹ (cell 3 to cell 2) due to the use of RCF and AF respectively in confined space tests.
claims.quote: The Sp for cell 3 to cell 4 and cell 3 to cell 2 in test No. 1 decreased from 7.84 ×10 −3 s −1 to 6.14 ×10 −3 s −1 in test No. 2, and from 7.84 ×10 −3 s −1 to 5.1 ×10 −3 s −1 in test No. 2.

claims.claim: For cell 3 heated by cell 1, the main heat transfer mode was conduction during stage I in all tests accounting for 68.6–78.8%, which changed to radiation with the proportion of 81.1–86.8% in stage II.
claims.quote: For cell 3 heated by cell 1, the main heat transfer mode was conduction during stage I in all tests, accounting for 68.6–78.8%, which changed to radiation with the proportion of 81.1–86.8% in stage II.

claims.claim: The temperature rise rate of cell 2 decreased from 0.98±0.03 °C/s to 0.64±0.06 °C/s when radiation heat was reduced using aluminum foil, while the temperature rise rate of cell 4 decreased from 0.87±0.08 °C/s to 0.73±0.07 °C/s when conduction heat was reduced using RCF, indicating radiation heat influences temperature rise rate more significantly.
claims.quote: The temperature rise rate of cell 2 with the reduction in radiation heat in test No. 2 decreased from 0.98 ±0.03 °C/s to 0.64 ±0.06 °C/s, compared with that in test No. 1. For cell 4, the temperature rise rate decreased from 0.87 ±0.08 °C/s to 0.73 ±0.07 °C/s as a result of the reduction in conduction heat absorbed by it.

claims.claim: Cell 2 underwent thermal runaway even when both aluminum foil and refractory ceramic fiber were used simultaneously to reduce radiation and conduction heat transfer, possibly because its temperature reached the self-accelerating decomposition temperature.
claims.quote: it is important to note that cell 2 underwent TR even though it was influenced by the use of AF and RCF. This may be induced by the condition where the temperature of cell 2 reached to the self-accelerating decomposition temperature (SADT).

claims.claim: The time-to-propagation of TR in a confined space was longer than that in a semi-confined space with the same experimental configuration, because the confined space reduces combustion efficiency and decreases heating intensity to neighboring cells.
claims.quote: the time-to-propagation of TR in a confined space is longer than that in a semi-confined space with the same configuration. This could be explained by the fact that the confined space affects the combustion efficiency of gases and further influences the maximum temperature that the cell will reach.

claims.claim: For a fully charged (100% SOC) 60 Ah LiFePO4/graphite pouch battery under an incident heat flux of 11.1 kW m-2, it takes 201 seconds from ignition to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: It takes 201 s from ignition to thermal runaway for fully charged battery under an incident heat ﬂux of 11.1 kW m-2.

claims.claim: Both incident heat flux and SOC have a negative correlation with ignition time, meaning batteries at higher SOC and exposed to higher incident heat flux ignite faster.
claims.quote: both the incident heat ﬂux and SOC have a negative correlation with the ignition time. This indicates that the batteries at higher SOC and exposed to higher incident heat ﬂux are more vulnerable to be ignited.

claims.claim: Incident heat flux has a greater influence on ignition time than SOC: increasing heat flux from 6.5 to 11.1 kW m-2 decreases ignition time by hundreds of seconds, while increasing SOC from 0 to 100% decreases it by only dozens of seconds.
claims.quote: the ignition time decreases by hundreds of seconds as incident heat ﬂux increases from 6.5 to 11.1 kW m-2, while it decreases by only dozens of seconds as SOC rises from 0 to 100% under the same radiation exposure.

claims.claim: Peak HRR values for 60 Ah LiFePO4 batteries under incident heat flux of 11.1 kW m-2 are 2.9 kW, 20.1 kW, and 53.3 kW for 0%, 50%, and 100% SOC, respectively.
claims.quote: The peak HRR values are 2.9, 20.1, and 53.3 kW for batteries with 0, 50, and 100% SOC, respectively.

claims.claim: For 100% SOC batteries, peak HRR decreases with increasing incident heat flux: peak HRR values are 82.3 kW, 75.1 kW, and 53.3 kW at incident heat fluxes of 6.5, 8.8, and 11.1 kW m-2, respectively, because higher heat flux causes more violent ejection leading to incomplete combustion.
claims.quote: the peak HRR values are 53.3, 75.1, and 82.3 kW for 100% SOC batteries under incident heat ﬂuxes of 11.1, 8.8, and 6.5 kW m-2, respectively. It is found that the peak HRR values decrease with ascending incident heat ﬂux for batteries at the same SOC. This can be explained by the fact that batteries under higher heat ﬂux experience a more violent ejection when thermal runaway occurs, which results in incomplete combustion and chemical reactions.

claims.claim: The stable combustion stage (Stage II) after ignition lasts 201 s, 523 s, and 671 s for 100%, 50%, and 0% SOC batteries, respectively, under an incident heat flux of 11.1 kW m-2, with higher SOC entering violent burning faster.
claims.quote: The phenomenon lasts for 201 s, 523 s, and 671 s at 100, 50, and 0% SOC, respectively. This reveals that the inner reaction processes in this period are relatively stable and peaceful. Nevertheless, the sample at higher SOC enters the violent burning process faster because of the higher reactivity of cathode and anode materials toward external heating.

claims.claim: Pouch lithium ion batteries without a safety valve undergo a stable small-flame combustion stage after ignition rather than immediate jet fire, because without a safety valve inner pressure causes slow gas release rather than instant violent ejection.
claims.quote: the pouch lithium ion battery that is studied in this work has no safety valve, so when the inner pressure reaches a certain degree but not very high, some gases decomposed from electrolyte as well as the evaporating gases will be released slowly rather than instant violent ejection.

claims.claim: For batteries under lower incident heat flux, the time interval between ignition and thermal runaway is shorter, making them also very dangerous despite longer absolute times to ignition.
claims.quote: the batteries exposed to lower incident heat ﬂux are also very dangerous due to the shorter time interval between ignition and thermal runaway as summarized in Table 4.

claims.claim: The SEI layer decomposes and releases heat at 80–120 °C, and the separator begins to melt at about 170 °C, which can accelerate exothermic reactions during thermal abuse of LiFePO4/graphite batteries.
claims.quote: the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer decomposes and release heat at 80–120 °C. At elevated temperature, the intercalated lithium in anode will react with electrolyte after losing the protection of SEI layer. Then the separator begins to melt at about 170 °C, which can accelerate the exothermic reactions.

claims.claim: In the absence of an igniter or high-temperature heat source close to the battery, released combustible gases may accumulate and cause a gas explosion upon delayed ignition in a confined enclosure.
claims.quote: In case, there is no igniter or high-temperature heat source that is very close to the battery, the releasing gases are hard to be ignited and a gas explosion may occur once a delayed ignition of vented combustible battery gases mixed with air accumulated in a conﬁned enclosure.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway of LiFeO4 battery under external heating alone does not cause combustion; flammable and explosive gases are generated but not ignited without an ignition source.
claims.quote: it can be concluded that thermal runaway of LiFeO4 battery used in this study does not cause combustion. Although there are lots of gases being generated, the flammable and explosive gases are not ignited without ignition sources.

claims.claim: Under external heating-ESC coupling abuse, the high-temperature electric conductor (exceeding 1000°C due to Joule heat) serves as the ignition source for flammable and explosive gases released after safety valve opening.
claims.quote: The temperature of the electric conductor is higher than 1000°C owing to Joule heat. The electric conductor is seen as ignition source. The explosive gas mixture is ignited.

claims.claim: External heating contributes much more to the accumulated heat of the battery before safety valve opening compared to the external short circuit (ESC) contribution under coupling abuse.
claims.quote: The external heating contributes much more to accumulated heat of battery at the moment of safety valve open. This means that external heating plays the key role in the thermal runaway and combustion of battery under external heating-ESC coupling abuse.

claims.claim: Under external heating-ESC coupling abuse, the time for safety valve opening decreases and the maximum battery temperature decreases compared to external heating alone, due to SOC reduction increasing thermal stability.
claims.quote: Compared to external heating, time for the safety valve open decreases, and the maximum temperature of battery decreases much under external heating-ESC coupling abuse... Thermal stability of battery increases owing to the decrease of SOC under external heating-ESC coupling abuse.

claims.claim: With increasing external resistance in the ESC circuit, combustion duration decreases and is less violent, but exothermic side reactions increase after thermal runaway.
claims.quote: With the increase of external resistance, the combustion lasts shorter and is less violent, but the exothermic side reactions increase after thermal runaway.

claims.claim: With increasing ESC triggering time (i.e., later triggering), combustion lasts longer but exothermic side reactions decrease after thermal runaway.
claims.quote: With the increase of triggering time of ESC, the combustion lasts longer, but the exothermic side reactions decrease after thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Internal short circuit in the LiFeO4 battery occurred when T1 reached 196.46°C, coinciding with fast voltage decrease, consistent with separator melting reported to begin at 120–135°C.
claims.quote: T1 is 196.46°C at the moment of fast decreasing voltage. It is reported that the separator starts to melt and shrink at the temperature range from 120 to 135°C. This also supports that the separator is destroyed, which induces internal short circuit.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway of the LiFeO4 battery under external heating was triggered when T1 reached 210.17°C and the temperature increasing rate exceeded 1°C/s.
claims.quote: At stage 3, T1 reaches 210.17°C, the temperature increasing rate is higher than 1°C/s, and thermal runway occurs.

claims.claim: In Case 1 (5 mΩ external resistance, ESC triggered simultaneously with external heating), the depth of discharge reached 65.56% when discharging current decreased to 0 A, and thermal runaway did not occur despite violent exothermic side reactions.
claims.quote: The depth of discharge (DOD) is 65.56% when the discharging decreases to 0 A. The thermal stability of battery decreases much compared to 100% SOC. The exothermic side reactions of battery decrease... However, thermal runaway does not occur.

claims.claim: LiFeO4 battery produces no sparks during thermal runaway unlike ternary batteries, indicating less electrode material detachment from the current collector.
claims.quote: Compared to ternary battery, there is no sparks occurring for LiFeO4 battery. This means that less electrode materials fall off the current collector.

claims.claim: In most studies, external short circuit (ESC) alone does not trigger thermal runaway of a battery, but ESC can trigger thermal runaway in aged batteries whose capacity, resistance, and material have changed.
claims.quote: In most studies, ESC does not trigger thermal runaway of battery. However, the capacity, resistance and material of aged battery change. ESC can trigger thermal runaway for aged battery.

claims.claim: The flame color during fast discharging is saffron yellow and changes to white after discharging current decreases to 0 A, indicating a temperature increase of the flame after discharge cessation.
claims.quote: The color of flame is saffron yellow during fast discharging. It changes to white after discharging current decreases to 0 A.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of thermal runaway (T2) obtained in the oven test is 48.1°C lower than that obtained in the adiabatic thermal runaway (ARC) test for a 24 Ah Li(Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3)O2/graphite pouch cell.
claims.quote: The onset temperature of thermal runaway (T2) obtained in the oven test is 48.1 ◦C lower than that obtained in the adiabatic thermal runaway test.

claims.claim: The maximum thermal runaway temperature (T3) at the battery surface is approximately half that at internal positions (center, JR_a, JR_b) under adiabatic test conditions.
claims.quote: the maximum thermal runaway temperature (T3) at the surface is approximately half that at the other positions.

claims.claim: Under adiabatic conditions, the maximum temperature rates at internal jelly roll positions (JR_a and JR_b) are approximately an order of magnitude higher than those measured at the center and surface.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature rates of JR_a and JR_b are approximately an order of magnitude higher than those at the center and surface.

claims.claim: When battery thickness increases to 10 cm during an oven test, the difference in T2 values between JR_a and the center exceeds 20% of the center value.
claims.quote: The difference in T2 values between JR_a and the center is the largest, exceeding 20% of the center value.

claims.claim: In oven tests, the sequence in which thermal runaway is triggered changes from center-first to surface-first as oven temperature increases, causing T2 at the surface to increase and T2 at the center to decrease with increasing oven temperature.
claims.quote: The sequence by which TR was triggered inside the battery changed from center → surface to surface → center with the increase in oven temperature, leading to an increase in T2 at the surface and a decrease in T2 at the center.

claims.claim: The critical temperature for mass gas generation is set at 109°C in the model; when jelly roll core temperatures reach this value, gas thermal resistance is added to simulate external or internal battery swelling.
claims.quote: The critical temperature for mass gas generation is set as 109 ◦C in this model. When the core temperatures of the JR_a and JR_d reach the critical temperature of gas generation, Rgas_out is added to simulate the external swelling of the battery.

claims.claim: Battery rupture is simulated when the temperature rate of any jelly roll node reaches 20°C/min, at which point all gas thermal resistances (Rgas_out, Rgas_in_1, Rgas_in_2) are reduced.
claims.quote: As the temperature rate of the JR_j reaches the critical value (20 ◦C/min) for TR, Rgas_out, Rgas_in_1, and Rgas_in_2 are reduced to simulate the battery rupture.

claims.claim: Under oven test conditions with battery thickness less than 5 cm, the TR temperature vs. time profiles at all four positions (surface, center, JR_a, JR_b) are superimposed, but a relatively large temperature difference occurs when thickness is greater than 10 cm.
claims.quote: The TR temperature vs. time profiles obtained at the four positions are superimposed when the battery thickness is less than 5 cm. In contrast, a relatively large temperature difference occurs when the thickness is larger than 10 cm.

claims.claim: Under adiabatic conditions, T2 at different positions remains almost constant as battery thickness increases, whereas T3 increases at internal positions (JR_a, JR_b, center) but decreases at the surface by approximately 30°C.
claims.quote: T2 at different positions remains almost constant with the increase in thickness... T3 at the surface shows a different trend and decreases by approximately 30 ◦C with the increase in battery thickness.

claims.claim: For oven tests with battery thickness greater than 5 cm, the thermocouple T2 value measured at the center is not caused by internal heat generation but results from large-scale high-temperature heat transfer from JR_a, making it an unreliable indicator of true thermal runaway onset.
claims.quote: the values at the center and JR_b are not caused by the heat generated inside the battery, but instead result from the large-scale high-temperature heat transfer.

claims.claim: During SEI decomposition, the temperature of lithium-ion batteries can reach up to 80 to 120°C, and the meta-stable substance in the SEI will decompose and produce CO2, O2, and some flammable hydrocarbon.
claims.quote: During SEI decomposition, the temperature of lithium-ion batteries can reach up to 80 to 120°C, and the meta-stable substance in the SEI will decompose and produce CO2, O2, and some flammable hydrocarbon.

claims.claim: The total amount of venting gas during thermal runaway is linearly related to the capacity of the lithium-ion battery, with an approximate formula of Vvent (L) = 1.961 × Ccell (Ah).
claims.quote: it is found that the total amount of venting gas is linearly related to the capacity of the lithium-ion battery, with an approximate formula of Vvent (L) ¼ 1.961 × Ccell (Ah).

claims.claim: The gas sensor can detect a thermal runaway fault signal around 7–8 minutes before the actual occurrence of thermal runaway at around 10 minutes, which is approximately 2 minutes earlier than the voltage sensor and 7 minutes earlier than the temperature sensor.
claims.quote: the gas sensor can detect the fault signal around 7–8 min before the actual occurrence of thermal runaway at around 10 min, which is around 2 min earlier than the voltage sensor and 7 min earlier than the temperature sensor.

claims.claim: The SEI layer is reported to be thermally stable up to around 100°C; decomposition may occur above this temperature, leading to deactivation and/or total failure of a battery.
claims.quote: Since SEI is reported to be thermally stable up to around 100°C, decomposition may occur above this temperature, leading to the deactivation and/or total failure of a battery.

claims.claim: The maximum internal core temperature of a lithium-ion battery during thermal runaway is nearly 500°C higher than the surface temperature.
claims.quote: It is found that the maximum internal core temperature is nearly 500°C higher than the surface temperature of the lithium-ion battery during thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The impedance phase shift Φ experiences a significant increase from 0° to 35° when the surface temperature reaches 128°C during thermal runaway, while the surface temperature is still rising slowly, indicating that impedance phase shift is a better indicator for monitoring gas venting and thermal runaway compared to surface temperature.
claims.quote: When Tsurf reaches 128°C, however, Φ experiences a significant increase, from 0° to 35°, but the surface temperature is still rising slowly, indicating the impedance phase shift Φ is a much better indicator for monitoring the gas venting and the thermal runaway process when compared to the surface temperature Tsurf.

claims.claim: In prior EIS-based internal temperature monitoring studies, temperatures greater than 65°C had not been successfully tracked, limiting the potential to pinpoint solid-electrolyte interphase decomposition above 100°C or predict impending battery failure.
claims.quote: In these previous studies, temperatures greater than 65°C have not been successfully tracked, limiting the potential to pinpoint the inter-phase decomposition of the solid-electrolyte above 100°C or predict any impending battery failure.

claims.claim: For a commercial PP/PE/PP three-layer separator, when the internal temperature of a lithium-ion battery rises above approximately 130°C, the porous PE layer will partially melt, blocking the pores of the separator and preventing lithium ion migration, while the PP layer provides mechanical support to avoid an internal short-circuit.
claims.quote: When the internal temperature of a lithium-ion battery rises above the critical temperature (around 130°C), the porous PE layer will partially melt, blocking the pores of the separator and preventing the migration of lithium ions in the electrolyte, while the PP layer provides the mechanical support necessary to avoid an internal short-circuit fault.

claims.claim: The emission of CO and H2 lags behind DEC during the thermal runaway process, so a SnO2-based ceramic semiconductor gas sensor detecting electrolyte vapor (DEC and DMC) can detect thermal runaway earlier than a Figaro gas sensor that only detects CO and H2.
claims.quote: This mechanism is able to detect a thermal runaway even earlier than the Figaro gas sensor, since the emission of CO and H2 lags behind the DEC during the thermal runaway process, while the Figaro gas sensor is only able to detect the former two gases.

claims.claim: The difference between the internal temperature and the surface temperature of a lithium-ion battery under a large discharge rate can be as much as 20°C.
claims.quote: it is observed that the difference between the internal temperature and the surface temperature can be as much as 20°C.

claims.claim: Li-ion (Li-ion) cells subjected to electrical abuse—overcharge and external short-circuit in particular—exhibit a rapid increase in cell temperature that could potentially lead to catastrophic disassembly of the cell.
claims.quote: Lithium-ion (Li-ion) cells that are subjected to electrical abuse, overcharge and external short-circuit in particular, exhibit a rapid increase in cell temperature that could potentially lead to catastrophic disassembly of the cell.

claims.claim: All prismatic Li-ion cells studied were found to use polyethylene-based shutdown (SD) separators that were irreversibly activated within a narrow temperature range between 130°C and 135°C.
claims.quote: All the cells studied were found to use polyethylene-based shutdown (SD) separators that were irreversibly activated within a narrow temperature range between 130 and 135°C.

claims.claim: In some prismatic Li-ion cells, irreversible cutoff was also provided by a current interrupt device (CID) or a thermal fuse, and both of these devices had a circuit-breaker effect, causing the impedance of the cell to rise infinitely and the OCV to drop to zero.
claims.quote: In some cells irreversible cutoff was also provided by a current interrupt device (CID) or a thermal fuse. Both these devices had a circuit-breaker effect, causing the impedance of the cell to rise infinitely and the OCV to drop to zero.

claims.claim: The impedance of cell A3 reached a maximum at around 175°C, and at temperatures above 175°C the impedance dropped back down by more than one order of magnitude, behavior typical of polyethylene separators and indicative of separator meltdown.
claims.quote: The impedance of cell A3 (Fig. 2) reached a maximum at around 175°C. At temperatures above 175°C the impedance dropped back down by more than one order of magnitude. This behavior too is typical of polyethylene separators and is indicative of separator meltdown (Sep MD).

claims.claim: In almost all cases, separators are not able to shut the cell down completely; therefore, an overcharged cell could continue to charge at lower currents even after the shutdown event, rendering the cell a potential hazard if not disposed of immediately and safely.
claims.quote: Second, in almost all cases separators are not able to shut the cell down completely. Therefore, an overcharged cell could continue to charge at lower currents even after the shutdown event, rendering the cell a potential hazard if not disposed of immediately and safely.

claims.claim: Minichannel cooling at cell level cannot prevent thermal runaway in a single cell even when the flow rate is as high as 10.0 L/min.
claims.quote: the minichannel cooling system could not prevent the thermal runaway in a battery cell even when the flow rate is as high as 10.0 L/min

claims.claim: When battery temperature exceeds the threshold of approximately 120°C, decomposition of jelly-roll materials (thermal abuse) is triggered, which further accelerates heat generation.
claims.quote: when the battery temperature reaches a threshold (~ 120 ℃), the decomposition of battery materials (referred to as thermal abuse) takes places, which further accelerates the heat generation

claims.claim: Without cooling, the maximum battery temperature peaks at approximately 950°C at 150 seconds after nail penetration triggers thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the temperature starts to increase rapidly because of the thermal abuse and peaks around 950 °C at 150 s

claims.claim: At a flow rate of 0.1 L/min, thermal runaway propagates to the adjacent battery (battery 2), causing its maximum temperature to reach 800°C.
claims.quote: When the flow rate is 0.1 L/min, the thermal runaway on battery 2 is also triggered due to the heat transferred from battery 1. As a result, the maximum temperature of battery 2 reaches 800 ℃.

claims.claim: At a flow rate of 10.0 L/min per cell, the maximum temperature of the adjacent battery (battery 2) can be maintained below 60°C, preventing thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: When the flow rate is increased to 10.0 L/min, the maximum temperature of battery 2 can be maintained below 60 ℃.

claims.claim: Increasing nail penetration depth from 20 mm to 60 mm causes thermal runaway to occur at an earlier stage and results in higher peak battery temperature.
claims.quote: when the penetration depth increases from 20 mm to 60 mm, the thermal runaway occurs at earlier stage and the battery reaches higher temperature

claims.claim: Increasing nail diameter from 3 mm to 5 mm causes thermal runaway to occur at a faster rate and results in higher peak battery temperature, because greater nail surface area generates more heat via short circuit.
claims.quote: When the nail diameter increases from 3 mm to 5 mm, the thermal runaway takes place at much faster speed and the battery reaches higher temperature... This is because more heat will be generated over the nail surface due to short circuit.

claims.claim: At flow rates of 0.1 L/min and 1.0 L/min, the maximum water temperature exceeds the boiling point of 100°C, which can cause local pressure increase and damage to the minichannel cooling system.
claims.quote: For flow rate 0.1 L/min and 1.0 L/min, the maximum water temperature is larger than the boiling point (100 ℃)... the boiling inside the minichannels can cause a huge local pressure increase, which can damage the minichannel cooling system.

claims.claim: The SEI decomposition reaction occurs first at approximately 60 seconds when battery temperature reaches 120°C, followed by the negative-electrolyte reaction at ~80 s (150°C), the positive-electrolyte reaction at ~100 s (180°C), and the electrolyte decomposition at ~110 s (200°C).
claims.quote: the SEI decomposition occurs first at 60 s when the temperature reaches 120 ℃. Following that is the negative-electrolyte reaction, which takes place at around 80 s when the temperature increases to 150 ℃. The positive-electrolyte reaction happens at 100 s when the temperature is 180 ℃... The electrolyte decomposition reaction takes places last at 110 s when the temperature reaches about 200 ℃.

claims.claim: The pressure drop for ethylene glycol coolant at 10.0 L/min is approximately 6 times higher than that for water at the same flow rate, due to the much higher viscosity of ethylene glycol.
claims.quote: The pressure drop in the case of ethylene glycol, however, is about 6 times higher than that in the case of water due to much higher viscosity of ethylene glycol.

claims.claim: Preventing thermal runaway propagation at battery pack level (80 modules) would require approximately 2400 L/min coolant flow rate and 312 W pumping power, which exceeds the capacity of a conventional radiator (~200 L/min) and is deemed infeasible.
claims.quote: If the control of the coolant flow rate can only be used at battery pack level, coolant with nearly 2400 L/min (30.0 L/min × 80 modules) flow rate and pumping power of 312 W (3.9 W × 80 modules) will be required... This is infeasible because the flow rate is too high for a conventional radiator to cool down the coolant.

claims.claim: Experimental results from Feng et al. showed that 12% of the total heat released during thermal runaway in one battery is sufficient to trigger thermal runaway in an adjacent battery.
claims.quote: Their results showed that 12% of the total heat released in thermal runaway could trigger the thermal runaway of the adjacent battery.

claims.claim: The parallel-series connection types (4P3S vs 3P4S vs no electrical connections) of a 12-cell lithium-ion battery module had no significant influence on TR propagation behaviour when considering maximum temperature and propagation time.
claims.quote: Considering the maximum temperature and propagation time, the parallel-series connection types of the battery module appeared to have no significant influence on the TR propagation behaviour.

claims.claim: Electricity transfer from adjacent normal cells to the TR cell during thermal runaway propagation in parallel-connected submodules led to a maximum temperature increase of approximately 28.2°C in the most severe condition, calculated via an equivalent circuit model.
claims.quote: The increase in temperature due to the electrical transfer was calculated to be 28.2 °C, which is low compared with the maximum temperature (~940 °C in the propagation process).
